title: "STAT4310 Regression Project"
author: "Thomas Linden, Louis Mestas, Luis Garcia, Emanuel Padilla, Eugene Kazi, Edgar Pineda"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    theme: flatly
    highlight-style: tango
  # docx:
  #   toc: true
  #   toc-depth: 3
  #   highlight-style: tango
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: true
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| code-fold: false
#| include: false

# This setup chunk does all background work for the report:
#   1. load packages,
#   2. define reusable helper functions,
#   3. run the HousePrices analysis,
#   4. run the CASchools analysis,
#   5. pull/analyze the finance data.
# The sections later in the file mostly display objects created here.
```

# Project Setup and Reproducibility

## Libraries

```{r}
#| label: setup-libs
#| code-fold: true
#| warning: false

# â”€â”€ Core Libraries â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€

library(AER)            # Applied Econometrics with R â€” CASchools dataset
library(MASS)           # Modern Applied Statistics â€” stepAIC() for model selection
library(car)            # Companion to Applied Regression â€” vif(), linearHypothesis()
library(lmtest)         # Regression diagnostic tests â€” bptest() (Breusch-Pagan), dwtest() (Durbin-Watson)
library(ModelMetrics)   # Model accuracy metrics â€” rmse(), mae() for train/test evaluation
library(broom)          # Tidy model output â€” tidy(), glance(), augment() for lm() summaries
library(knitr)          # R Markdown rendering â€” kable() for formatted tables
library(quantmod)       # Quantitative finance â€” getSymbols() to pull NYSE/S&P 500 price data
```

## Functions

Below are the custom functions used across the HousePrices and CASchools analyses, as well as the finance section. These include metric calculations, diagnostic tests, model selection utilities, predictor transformation helpers, Box-Cox transformation tools, and rolling market statistic computations. A consistent random seed, (`4310`) is set at the beginning to ensure reproducibility across all random processes in the project.

### MSE, RMSE, MAE and RÂ˛ Calculation

The following metrics are used to evaluate model fit and out-of-sample predictive accuracy.

**Mean Squared Error (MSE)** â€” penalizes larger errors more heavily via squaring:

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
$$

**Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** â€” restores units to the original scale of $y$:

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}
$$

**Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** â€” robust alternative; treats all errors equally:

$$
\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |y_i - \hat{y}_i|
$$

**Out-of-Sample RÂ˛** â€” measures variance explained on held-out test data:

$$
R^2_{\text{test}} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2} = 1 - \frac{\text{SS}_{\text{Res}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{Tot}}}
$$

where $y_i$ are observed values, $\hat{y}_i$ are model predictions, and $\bar{y}$ is the mean of observed values.

```{r}
#| label: setup-function1
#| code-fold: true

# Set a consistent seed for reproducibility across all random processes
set.seed(4310)

# â”€â”€ Custom Functions â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
# These helper functions keep the analysis code shorter and make the same
# methods reusable across HousePrices, CASchools, and the finance section.

# Computes MSE, RMSE, MAE, and out-of-sample RÂ˛ for a set of actual vs. predicted values
get_metrics <- function(actual, predicted) {
  data.frame(
    MSE = mse(predicted, actual),
    RMSE = rmse(predicted, actual),
    MAE = mae(predicted, actual),
    Test_R2 = 1 - sum((actual - predicted)^2) /
      sum((actual - mean(actual))^2)
  )
}
```

### Diagnostic Tests and Model Summary Tables

The following tests assess the three core regression assumptions on the residuals
$\hat{\varepsilon}_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$.

**Shapiro-Wilk Test (Normality)** â€” tests whether residuals follow a normal distribution.
The test statistic is:

$$
W = \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \hat{\varepsilon}_{(i)}\right)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{\varepsilon}_i - \bar{\varepsilon})^2}
$$

where $\hat{\varepsilon}_{(i)}$ are the ordered residuals and $a_i$ are constants derived
from the expected order statistics of a normal distribution. $H_0$: residuals are normal.

**Breusch-Pagan Test (Constant Variance / Homoscedasticity)** â€” detects whether
error variance is constant across fitted values. The auxiliary regression is:

$$
\hat{\varepsilon}_i^2 = \gamma_0 + \gamma_1 x_{i1} + \cdots + \gamma_k x_{ik} + \nu_i
$$

The test statistic $BP = nR^2_{\text{aux}} \sim \chi^2(k)$ under $H_0: \gamma_1 = \cdots = \gamma_k = 0$
(homoscedasticity).

**Durbin-Watson Test (Independence / No Autocorrelation)** â€” detects first-order
serial correlation in residuals:

$$
DW = \frac{\sum_{i=2}^{n}(\hat{\varepsilon}_i - \hat{\varepsilon}_{i-1})^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\hat{\varepsilon}_i^2}
$$

$DW \approx 2$ indicates no autocorrelation. $DW < 2$ suggests positive autocorrelation;
$DW > 2$ suggests negative. $H_0$: $\rho = 0$ (independent errors).

```{r}
#| label: setup-function2
#| code-fold: true

# Runs Shapiro-Wilk (normality), Breusch-Pagan (constant variance), and
# Durbin-Watson (independence) tests; returns a tidy p-value table
get_diag <- function(model) {
  data.frame(
    Test = c("Shapiro-Wilk", "Breusch-Pagan", "Durbin-Watson"),
    "P Value" = trimws(format.pval(c(
      shapiro.test(residuals(model))$p.value,
      bptest(model)$p.value,
      dwtest(model)$p.value
    ), digits = 3, eps = 1e-16))
  )
}
```

### Coefficient Term Table

After fitting, non-intercept coefficients are extracted and ranked by
significance. The fitted multiple linear regression model is:

$$
\hat{y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_{i1} + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_p x_{ip}
$$

Each coefficient $\hat{\beta}_j$ is tested individually via a two-sided
$t$-test (Montgomery et al., Eq. 3.29):

$$
H_0: \beta_j = 0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_a: \beta_j \neq 0
$$

$$
t_j = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{\text{se}(\hat{\beta}_j)} \sim t_{n-p-1},
\qquad \text{se}(\hat{\beta}_j) = \sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 C_{jj}}
$$

where $C_{jj}$ is the $j$-th diagonal element of $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$
and $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \text{MS}_\text{Res}$. This is a **partial test** â€” the
contribution of $x_j$ given all other predictors already in the model.

```{r}
#| label: setup-function3
#| code-fold: true

get_term_table <- function(model) {
  out <- tidy(model)
  out <- out[out$term != "(Intercept)", c("term", "estimate", "p.value")]
  out <- out[order(out$p.value), ]
  out$estimate <- sprintf("%.4f", out$estimate)
  out$p.value <- trimws(format.pval(out$p.value, digits = 3, eps = 1e-16))
  names(out) <- c("Term", "Estimate", "P Value")
  rownames(out) <- NULL
  out
}
```

### Sensitivity Table Formatting

```{r}
#| label: setup-function4
#| code-fold: true

# Formats held-out test RMSE for the sensitivity tables. If AIC and CV choose
# the same final step, we print one RMSE instead of duplicating the same number.
format_test_rmse <- function(aic_step, cv_step, aic_rmse, cv_rmse) {
  if (identical(aic_step, cv_step)) {
    sprintf("%.2f (same for AIC and CV)", aic_rmse)
  } else {
    sprintf("AIC %.2f, CV %.2f", aic_rmse, cv_rmse)
  }
}
```

### Backward Stepwise Model Selection (AIC Ladder)

Starting from the full model, at each step the predictor whose removal yields
the lowest **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)** is dropped. For ordinary least
squares regression (Montgomery 5E, Ch. 10):

$$
\text{AIC} = n \ln\!\left(\frac{\text{SS}_\text{Res}}{n}\right) + 2p
$$

where $n$ is the number of observations and $p$ is the number of parameters
(including the intercept). AIC penalizes model complexity â€” adding a predictor
must reduce $\text{SS}_\text{Res}$ enough to offset the $+2$ penalty term.

At each step $s$, the algorithm evaluates all $p_s$ candidate single-term
removals and selects the one minimizing AIC:

$$
\text{removed}_s = \arg\min_{j} \; \text{AIC}\!\left(\mathcal{M}_s \setminus \{x_j\}\right)
$$

This produces a **nested ladder** of models $\mathcal{M}_0 \supset \mathcal{M}_1
\supset \cdots \supset \mathcal{M}_{p-1}$.

```{r}
#| label: setup-function5
#| code-fold: true

# Builds the model-selection ladder used in the CV plots. Step 0 is the full
# model. At each later step, the function tries dropping each remaining
# predictor and keeps the drop that gives the lowest AIC. CV then evaluates
# every model on this AIC-based ladder.
build_backward_ladder <- function(model) {
  models <- list(model)
  steps <- data.frame(
    step_num = 0,
    step = "Step 0",
    predictors = length(attr(terms(model), "term.labels")),
    aic = AIC(model),
    removed = NA_character_,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )

  current_model <- model

  while (length(attr(terms(current_model), "term.labels")) > 1) {
    current_terms <- attr(terms(current_model), "term.labels")

    candidate_models <- lapply(current_terms, function(term_name) {
      update(current_model, as.formula(paste(". ~ . -", term_name)))
    })

    candidate_aic <- sapply(candidate_models, AIC)
    best_idx <- which.min(candidate_aic)
    current_model <- candidate_models[[best_idx]]

    models[[length(models) + 1]] <- current_model
    steps <- rbind(
      steps,
      data.frame(
        step_num = nrow(steps),
        step = paste("Step", nrow(steps)),
        predictors = length(attr(terms(current_model), "term.labels")),
        aic = AIC(current_model),
        removed = current_terms[best_idx],
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      )
    )
  }

  list(models = models, steps = steps)
}
```

### Repeated $k$-Fold Cross-Validation

Each ladder step is evaluated via repeated $k$-fold CV ($k = 5$, repeats $= 6$,
yielding 30 RMSE estimates per step). The data are partitioned into $k$ folds;
the model is trained on $k-1$ folds and validated on the held-out fold:

$$
\text{RMSE}_{r,f} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{|\mathcal{V}_f|}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{V}_f}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}
$$

where $\mathcal{V}_f$ is the index set of fold $f$ in repeat $r$. The mean CV
RMSE across all folds and repeats identifies the optimal ladder step:

$$
\overline{\text{RMSE}}_{\text{CV}} = \frac{1}{R \cdot k}\sum_{r=1}^{R}\sum_{f=1}^{k} \text{RMSE}_{r,f}
$$

```{r}
#| label: setup-function6
#| code-fold: true

# Runs repeated k-fold cross-validation for every model on the ladder. In this
# project k = 5 and repeats = 6, so each step gets 30 validation RMSE values
# for the boxplots and one mean RMSE for the selection line.
cv_model_ladder <- function(data, response_name, ladder, k = 5, repeats = 6, seed = 4310) {
  set.seed(seed)
  cv.results <- data.frame()

  for (r in 1:repeats) {
    folds <- sample(rep(1:k, length.out = nrow(data)))

    for (j in seq_along(ladder$models)) {
      form <- formula(ladder$models[[j]])

      for (fold in 1:k) {
        train.fold <- data[folds != fold, , drop = FALSE]
        valid.fold <- data[folds == fold, , drop = FALSE]

        fit <- lm(form, data = train.fold)
        pred <- predict(fit, newdata = valid.fold)
        actual <- valid.fold[[response_name]]

        cv.results <- rbind(
          cv.results,
          data.frame(
            step = ladder$steps$step[j],
            step_num = ladder$steps$step_num[j],
            predictors = ladder$steps$predictors[j],
            repeat_id = r,
            fold_id = fold,
            RMSE = sqrt(mean((actual - pred)^2)),
            MAE = mean(abs(actual - pred))
          )
        )
      }
    }
  }

  cv.results
}
```

### CV Summary

```{r}
#| label: setup-function7
#| code-fold: true

# Averages the fold-level CV results so we can identify the step with the
# lowest validation RMSE and draw the mean CV RMSE line on the ladder plots.
summarize_cv_results <- function(cv.results) {
  out <- aggregate(
    cbind(RMSE, MAE) ~ step + step_num + predictors,
    data = cv.results,
    FUN = mean
  )

  out$RMSE <- round(out$RMSE, 4)
  out$MAE <- round(out$MAE, 4)
  out[order(out$step_num), ]
}
```

### Bootstrap Coefficient Intervals

Empirical 95% confidence intervals are obtained by resampling $B = 200$ rows
with replacement from the cleaned training data and refitting the final model
each time. For each bootstrap replicate $b$:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{*(b)} = \left(\mathbf{X}^{*(b)\top}\mathbf{X}^{*(b)}\right)^{-1}
\mathbf{X}^{*(b)\top}\mathbf{y}^{*(b)}, \quad b = 1, \ldots, B
$$

where $(\mathbf{X}^{*(b)}, \mathbf{y}^{*(b)})$ is the $b$-th resample. The
empirical 95% interval for each $\beta_j$ is then the 2.5th and 97.5th
quantiles of the bootstrap distribution:

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%}(\beta_j) = \left[\hat{\beta}_j^{*(0.025)},\;
\hat{\beta}_j^{*(0.975)}\right]
$$

Unlike analytic intervals, bootstrap intervals are distribution-free and
robust to non-normality in the residuals.

```{r}
#| label: setup-function8
#| code-fold: true

# Bootstraps coefficient intervals by resampling rows from the cleaned training
# data, refitting the final model, and taking the 2.5% and 97.5% coefficient
# quantiles as empirical 95% intervals.
bootstrap_coef_table <- function(model, data, B = 200) {
  coef.names <- names(coef(model))

  boot.mat <- replicate(B, {
    idx <- sample(1:nrow(data), replace = TRUE)
    fit <- lm(formula(model), data = data[idx, , drop = FALSE])
    coef(fit)[coef.names]
  })

  if (is.null(dim(boot.mat))) {
    boot.mat <- matrix(boot.mat, nrow = length(coef.names))
  }

  out <- data.frame(
    term = coef.names,
    estimate = coef(model),
    lower = apply(boot.mat, 1, quantile, probs = 0.025, na.rm = TRUE),
    upper = apply(boot.mat, 1, quantile, probs = 0.975, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

  out[, 2:4] <- round(out[, 2:4], 4)
  rownames(out) <- NULL
  out
}

# Computes RMSE and held-out R2 for sensitivity checks. These are intentionally
# simple so the calculation is visible.
feature_rmse <- function(actual, predicted) {
  sqrt(mean((actual - predicted)^2))
}

feature_test_r2 <- function(actual, predicted) {
  1 - sum((actual - predicted)^2) / sum((actual - mean(actual))^2)
}
```

### Collinearity Diagnostics â€” VIF and Correlation Screening

**Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)** measures how much the variance of
$\hat{\beta}_j$ is inflated by linear dependence with other predictors
(Montgomery 5E, Â§9.4.2):

$$
\text{VIF}_j = C_{jj} = \frac{1}{1 - R^2_j}
$$

where $R^2_j$ is the coefficient of determination from regressing $x_j$
on all remaining predictors. $\text{VIF}_j \approx 1$ indicates near
orthogonality; values exceeding 5â€“10 signal problematic collinearity.

For categorical predictors with $d_j > 1$ degrees of freedom, `car::vif()`
returns a **Generalized VIF (GVIF)** adjusted to a single-degree scale for
fair comparison:

$$
\text{GVIF}^{1/(2d_j)}_j
$$

As a complementary screen, pairwise Pearson correlations are computed for
all numeric predictors. Any pair exceeding $|\rho| \geq 0.90$ is flagged:

$$
\rho_{jk} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)(x_{ik} - \bar{x}_k)}
{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n(x_{ij}-\bar{x}_j)^2\sum_{i=1}^n(x_{ik}-\bar{x}_k)^2}}
$$

::: {.callout-note}
## Why VIF is a diagnostic, not a filter

Neither the VIF screen nor the correlation screen triggers automatic term
removal. AIC/CV decides whether collinear terms improve prediction.
Centered squared and interaction terms are correlated with their source
variables by construction, so a hard pre-filter would delete useful
engineered predictors before the selection criteria ever see them.
:::

```{r}
#| label: setup-function9
#| code-fold: true

# â”€â”€ Core diagnostic utilities â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
collapse_terms <- function(term_vec) {
  if (length(term_vec) == 0) {
    "None"
  } else {
    paste(term_vec, collapse = ", ")
  }
}

get_model_terms <- function(model) {
  attr(terms(model), "term.labels")
}

get_interaction_terms <- function(model) {
  grep(":", get_model_terms(model), value = TRUE)
}

make_formula <- function(response_name, term_vec) {
  reformulate(term_vec, response = response_name)
}

get_vif_table <- function(model) {
  vif.raw <- car::vif(model)

  if (is.matrix(vif.raw)) {
    out <- data.frame(
      Term = rownames(vif.raw),
      VIF = vif.raw[, "GVIF"]^(1 / (2 * vif.raw[, "Df"])),
      row.names = NULL
    )
  } else {
    out <- data.frame(
      Term = names(vif.raw),
      VIF = as.numeric(vif.raw),
      row.names = NULL
    )
  }

  out[order(-out$VIF), ]
}

get_high_correlation_pairs <- function(data, terms, cutoff = 0.90) {
  present.terms <- terms[terms %in% names(data)]
  numeric.terms <- present.terms[vapply(data[present.terms], is.numeric, logical(1))]

  if (length(numeric.terms) < 2) {
    return(data.frame(
      Variable_1 = character(),
      Variable_2 = character(),
      Correlation = numeric()
    ))
  }

  cor.mat <- cor(data[, numeric.terms, drop = FALSE], use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
  cor.idx <- which(abs(cor.mat) >= cutoff & upper.tri(cor.mat), arr.ind = TRUE)

  if (nrow(cor.idx) == 0) {
    return(data.frame(
      Variable_1 = character(),
      Variable_2 = character(),
      Correlation = numeric()
    ))
  }

  out <- data.frame(
    Variable_1 = rownames(cor.mat)[cor.idx[, 1]],
    Variable_2 = colnames(cor.mat)[cor.idx[, 2]],
    Correlation = cor.mat[cor.idx],
    row.names = NULL
  )
  out[order(-abs(out$Correlation)), ]
}

# â”€â”€ Screening wrapper and display formatters â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
diagnose_terms_for_collinearity <- function(response_name, terms, data, corr_cutoff = 0.90) {
  formula <- make_formula(response_name, terms)

  list(
    formula = formula,
    terms = terms,
    high_correlations = get_high_correlation_pairs(data, terms, cutoff = corr_cutoff),
    final_vif = get_vif_table(lm(formula, data = data)),
    corr_cutoff = corr_cutoff
  )
}

format_high_correlation_table <- function(screen.result) {
  out <- screen.result$high_correlations

  if (nrow(out) == 0) {
    return(data.frame(
      `Variable 1` = "None above cutoff",
      `Variable 2` = "",
      Correlation = "",
      check.names = FALSE
    ))
  }

  out$Correlation <- sprintf("%.3f", out$Correlation)
  names(out) <- c("Variable 1", "Variable 2", "Correlation")
  out
}

format_final_vif_table <- function(screen.result, max_rows = 10) {
  out <- head(screen.result$final_vif, max_rows)
  out$VIF <- sprintf("%.2f", out$VIF)
  names(out) <- c("Term", "Adjusted VIF")
  out
}

format_screen_summary <- function(screen.result, label) {
  data.frame(
    Stage = label,
    `High Correlation Pairs` = nrow(screen.result$high_correlations),
    `Max Adjusted VIF` = sprintf("%.2f", max(screen.result$final_vif$VIF)),
    `Terms Sent to AIC/CV` = length(screen.result$terms),
    `Pre-AIC Removals` = "None; AIC/CV selects from full centered set",
    check.names = FALSE
  )
}

get_center_values <- function(data, variables) {
  out <- lapply(variables, function(variable) {
    mean(data[[variable]], na.rm = TRUE)
  })
  names(out) <- variables
  out
}
```

### Predictor Transformations â€” HousePrices

Continuous predictors are mean-centered on the training set before
nonlinear and interaction terms are constructed. For predictor $x_j$:

$$
\tilde{x}_j = x_j - \bar{x}_j^{\text{train}}
$$

Centering reduces collinearity between main effects and their squares or
cross-products. The log and scaled lotsize transforms are applied first:

$$
\widetilde{\log(\text{lotsize})} = \log(x_{\text{lotsize}}) - \overline{\log(x_{\text{lotsize}})}^{\text{train}}
$$

$$
\widetilde{x}_{\text{lotsize}/1000} = \frac{x_{\text{lotsize}}}{1000} - \bar{x}_{\text{lotsize}/1000}^{\text{train}}
$$

Interaction terms are formed as products of centered variables:

$$
\tilde{x}_j \cdot \tilde{x}_k \quad \text{e.g.,} \quad
\widetilde{\text{bed}} \times \widetilde{\text{bath}}, \quad
\widetilde{\text{garage}} \times \mathbf{1}[\text{prefer} = \text{yes}]
$$

Training-set means $\bar{x}_j^{\text{train}}$ are reused for the test set
to prevent data leakage:

$$
\tilde{x}_j^{\text{test}} = x_j^{\text{test}} - \bar{x}_j^{\text{train}}
$$

```{r}
#| label: setup-function10
#| code-fold: true

# HousePrices transformed variables are deterministic row-level transformations.
# Centering values are estimated on the training set and reused for test
# rows and prediction examples to avoid leaking test-set information.
add_house_features <- function(data, centers = NULL) {
  data$log_lotsize <- log(data$lotsize)
  data$lotsize_1000 <- data$lotsize / 1000

  center.variables <- c(
    "lotsize", "bedrooms", "bathrooms", "stories", "garage",
    "log_lotsize", "lotsize_1000"
  )
  if (is.null(centers)) {
    centers <- get_center_values(data, center.variables)
  }

  data$lotsize_c <- data$lotsize - centers$lotsize
  data$bedrooms_c <- data$bedrooms - centers$bedrooms
  data$bathrooms_c <- data$bathrooms - centers$bathrooms
  data$stories_c <- data$stories - centers$stories
  data$garage_c <- data$garage - centers$garage
  data$log_lotsize_c <- data$log_lotsize - centers$log_lotsize
  data$lotsize_1000_c <- data$lotsize_1000 - centers$lotsize_1000
  data$lotsize_1000_c_sq <- data$lotsize_1000_c^2
  data$bed_bath_c <- data$bedrooms_c * data$bathrooms_c
  data$bath_story_c <- data$bathrooms_c * data$stories_c
  data$garage_prefer_c <- data$garage_c * as.integer(data$prefer == "yes")
  attr(data, "feature_centers") <- centers
  data
}
```

```{r}
#| label: setup-function11
#| code-fold: true

# Candidate predictor sets passed to the collinearity screen and AIC/CV ladder
house.base.terms <- c(
  "lotsize_c", "bedrooms_c", "bathrooms_c", "stories_c", "driveway",
  "recreation", "fullbase", "gasheat", "aircon", "garage_c", "prefer"
)
house.derived.terms <- c("log_lotsize_c", "lotsize_1000_c_sq")
house.interaction.terms <- c(
  "bed_bath_c", "bath_story_c", "garage_prefer_c",
  "log_lotsize_c:prefer", "log_lotsize_c:aircon", "garage_c:lotsize_1000_c"
)
```

### Predictor Transformations â€” CASchools

CASchools predictors follow the same mean-centering approach as HousePrices.
Squared terms capture socioeconomic nonlinearities (diminishing returns of
income, accelerating poverty effects), and interactions allow slopes to vary
by district grade-span type.

```{r}
#| label: setup-function12
#| code-fold: true

# CASchools predictors: split first, estimate centers on training data only,
# then create centered nonlinear and interaction terms from those training-set
# centers.
add_school_features <- function(data, centers = NULL) {
  center.variables <- c(
    "str", "calworks", "lunch", "computer.rate",
    "expenditure", "income", "english"
  )
  if (is.null(centers)) {
    centers <- get_center_values(data, center.variables)
  }

  data$str_c <- data$str - centers$str
  data$calworks_c <- data$calworks - centers$calworks
  data$lunch_c <- data$lunch - centers$lunch
  data$computer.rate_c <- data$computer.rate - centers$computer.rate
  data$expenditure_c <- data$expenditure - centers$expenditure
  data$income_c <- data$income - centers$income
  data$english_c <- data$english - centers$english
  data$income_c_sq <- data$income_c^2
  data$lunch_c_sq <- data$lunch_c^2
  data$english_c_sq <- data$english_c^2
  data$low_income_pressure_c <- data$lunch_c * data$english_c
  data$income_lunch_c <- data$income_c * data$lunch_c
  data$str_lunch_c <- data$str_c * data$lunch_c
  attr(data, "feature_centers") <- centers
  data
}
```

```{r}
#| label: setup-function13
#| code-fold: true

# Candidate predictor sets for the CASchools selection ladder
schools.base.terms <- c(
  "str_c", "calworks_c", "lunch_c", "computer.rate_c", "expenditure_c",
  "income_c", "english_c", "grades"
)
schools.derived.terms <- c("income_c_sq", "lunch_c_sq", "english_c_sq")
schools.interaction.terms <- c(
  "low_income_pressure_c", "income_lunch_c", "str_lunch_c",
  "lunch_c:grades", "income_c:grades", "english_c:grades", "str_c:grades"
)
```

### Box-Cox Power Transformation

When residuals show non-normality or heteroscedasticity, a Box-Cox
transformation of the response is applied. The power family is
(Montgomery 5E, Ch. 5):

$$
y^{(\lambda)} =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{y^\lambda - 1}{\lambda} & \lambda \neq 0 \\[6pt]
\ln(y) & \lambda = 0
\end{cases}
$$

The optimal $\lambda$ maximizes the profile log-likelihood:

$$
\ell(\lambda) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{\text{SS}_\text{Res}(\lambda)}{n}\right)
+ (\lambda - 1)\sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln(y_i)
$$

A 95% confidence interval for $\lambda$ uses the chi-squared cutoff
$\ell(\hat{\lambda}) - \frac{1}{2}\chi^2_{0.95,\,1}$. If $\lambda = 1$ falls
inside this interval, the untransformed model is retained.

**Smearing back-transform** corrects for Jensen's inequality bias when
converting predictions back to the original scale:

$$
\hat{y}_i = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}
g^{-1}\!\left(\hat{y}_i^{(\lambda)} + \hat{\varepsilon}_j^{(\lambda)}\right)
$$

where $g^{-1}$ is the inverse Box-Cox transform and
$\hat{\varepsilon}_j^{(\lambda)}$ are the transformed-scale residuals.

```{r}
#| label: setup-function14
#| code-fold: true

# Box-Cox utilities: estimate lambda, fit a transformed response model, then
# compare predictions after converting back to the original response scale.
boxcox_transform <- function(y, lambda) {
  if (abs(lambda) < 1e-8) {
    log(y)
  } else {
    (y^lambda - 1) / lambda
  }
}

boxcox_inverse <- function(z, lambda) {
  if (abs(lambda) < 1e-8) {
    exp(z)
  } else {
    base <- lambda * z + 1
    ifelse(base > 0, base^(1 / lambda), NA_real_)
  }
}

boxcox_profile <- function(model, lambda.seq = seq(-2, 2, by = 0.01)) {
  profile <- MASS::boxcox(model, lambda = lambda.seq, plotit = FALSE)
  max.loglik <- max(profile$y)
  cutoff <- max.loglik - 0.5 * qchisq(0.95, df = 1)
  plausible <- profile$x[profile$y >= cutoff]

  list(
    lambda = profile$x[which.max(profile$y)],
    lower = min(plausible),
    upper = max(plausible),
    identity.in.interval = 1 >= min(plausible) && 1 <= max(plausible),
    cutoff = cutoff,
    profile = data.frame(lambda = profile$x, logLik = profile$y)
  )
}

boxcox_smearing_predict <- function(fit, newdata, lambda) {
  transformed.fit <- as.numeric(predict(fit, newdata = newdata))
  transformed.residuals <- residuals(fit)

  vapply(
    transformed.fit,
    function(value) {
      mean(boxcox_inverse(value + transformed.residuals, lambda), na.rm = TRUE)
    },
    numeric(1)
  )
}

boxcox_fit_and_evaluate <- function(model, train.data, test.data, response.name, dataset.name) {
  profile <- boxcox_profile(model)
  lambda <- profile$lambda

  train.boxcox <- train.data
  train.boxcox$.boxcox_response <- boxcox_transform(train.boxcox[[response.name]], lambda)

  transformed.formula <- reformulate(
    get_model_terms(model),
    response = ".boxcox_response"
  )
  transformed.model <- lm(transformed.formula, data = train.boxcox)

  actual <- test.data[[response.name]]
  current.pred <- predict(model, newdata = test.data)
  naive.pred <- boxcox_inverse(
    as.numeric(predict(transformed.model, newdata = test.data)),
    lambda
  )
  smearing.pred <- boxcox_smearing_predict(transformed.model, test.data, lambda)

  comparison <- data.frame(
    Dataset = dataset.name,
    Model = c(
      "Current final",
      "Box-Cox naive back-transform",
      "Box-Cox smearing back-transform"
    ),
    Lambda = c(NA, lambda, lambda),
    Test_R2 = c(
      feature_test_r2(actual, current.pred),
      feature_test_r2(actual, naive.pred),
      feature_test_r2(actual, smearing.pred)
    ),
    Test_RMSE = c(
      feature_rmse(actual, current.pred),
      feature_rmse(actual, naive.pred),
      feature_rmse(actual, smearing.pred)
    ),
    Shapiro_P = c(
      shapiro.test(residuals(model))$p.value,
      shapiro.test(residuals(transformed.model))$p.value,
      shapiro.test(residuals(transformed.model))$p.value
    ),
    BP_P = c(
      bptest(model)$p.value,
      bptest(transformed.model)$p.value,
      bptest(transformed.model)$p.value
    )
  )

  list(
    profile = profile,
    model = transformed.model,
    comparison = comparison
  )
}
```

```{r}
#| label: setup-function15
#| code-fold: true

# Box-Cox display utilities: profile plot and formatted result tables
plot_boxcox_profile <- function(profile, title) {
  plot(
    profile$profile$lambda,
    profile$profile$logLik,
    type = "l",
    lwd = 2,
    col = "#123c55",
    xlab = "lambda",
    ylab = "Profile log-likelihood",
    main = title
  )
  abline(v = profile$lambda, col = "#0b6e4f", lwd = 2)
  abline(v = 1, col = "#b83227", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
  abline(h = profile$cutoff, col = "gray45", lwd = 2, lty = 3)
  legend(
    "bottomleft",
    legend = c("Best lambda", "lambda = 1", "Approx. 95% cutoff"),
    col = c("#0b6e4f", "#b83227", "gray45"),
    lty = c(1, 2, 3),
    lwd = 2,
    bty = "n",
    cex = 0.8
  )
}

boxcox_performance_table <- function(boxcox.result) {
  display <- boxcox.result$comparison
  display$Lambda <- ifelse(is.na(display$Lambda), NA, round(display$Lambda, 2))
  display$Test_R2 <- round(display$Test_R2, 4)
  display$Test_RMSE <- round(display$Test_RMSE, 2)
  display$Shapiro_P <- trimws(format.pval(display$Shapiro_P, digits = 3, eps = 1e-16))
  display$BP_P <- trimws(format.pval(display$BP_P, digits = 3, eps = 1e-16))
  names(display) <- c("Dataset", "Model", "Lambda", "Test R2", "Test RMSE", "Shapiro P", "BP P")
  display
}

boxcox_decision_table <- function(boxcox.result, decision) {
  data.frame(
    `Best Lambda` = round(boxcox.result$profile$lambda, 2),
    `Approx. 95% Lambda Interval` = paste0(
      "[", round(boxcox.result$profile$lower, 2), ", ",
      round(boxcox.result$profile$upper, 2), "]"
    ),
    `Lambda 1 In Interval` = boxcox.result$profile$identity.in.interval,
    Decision = decision,
    check.names = FALSE
  )
}
```

### CAPM Regression and Rolling Market Statistics

For the finance application, each stock's daily return is regressed on the
S&P 500 market return via the **Capital Asset Pricing Model**:

$$
r_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i \, r_{m,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t}
$$

where $r_{i,t}$ is stock $i$'s return on day $t$, $r_{m,t}$ is the market
return, $\alpha_i$ is Jensen's alpha (excess return), and $\beta_i$ measures
market sensitivity. $\beta > 1$ implies amplified market exposure; $\beta < 1$
implies defensiveness.

**Annualized return and risk** scale daily statistics to a yearly horizon
using 252 trading days:

$$
\mu_{\text{annual}} = 252 \cdot \bar{r}, \qquad
\sigma_{\text{annual}} = \sqrt{252} \cdot s_r
$$

**Rolling 60-day beta and correlation** capture time-varying market exposure.
For a trailing window $\mathcal{W}_t$ of 60 days:

$$
\hat{\beta}_{i,t} = \frac{\widehat{\text{Cov}}(r_i, r_m)_{\mathcal{W}_t}}
                         {\widehat{\text{Var}}(r_m)_{\mathcal{W}_t}},
\qquad
\hat{\rho}_{i,t} = \frac{\widehat{\text{Cov}}(r_i, r_m)_{\mathcal{W}_t}}
                         {s_{i,\mathcal{W}_t} \, s_{m,\mathcal{W}_t}}
$$

```{r}
#| label: setup-function16
#| code-fold: true

# Computes a rolling 60-day statistic against the S&P 500. With stat = "beta",
# it returns CAPM-style market sensitivity; with stat = "correlation", it
# returns the rolling strength of market co-movement.
rolling_market_stat <- function(stock_series, market_series, width = 60,
                                stat = c("beta", "correlation")) {
  stat <- match.arg(stat)

  zoo::rollapplyr(
    merge(stock = stock_series, market = market_series),
    width = width,
    by.column = FALSE,
    fill = NA,
    FUN = function(z) {
      z <- as.matrix(z)

      if (stat == "beta") {
        cov(z[, 1], z[, 2]) / var(z[, 2])
      } else {
        cor(z[, 1], z[, 2])
      }
    }
  )
}
```

## HousePrices Analysis Pipeline

```{r}
#| label: pipeline-house
#| code-fold: true
#| include: true

data("HousePrices", package = "AER")

# â”€â”€ Dataset 1: HousePrices analysis pipeline â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
# Goal: predict house sale price from physical and neighborhood features, then
# compare AIC selection, CV selection, Cook filtering, bootstrap intervals, and
# prediction intervals.

# Keep a separate test set before centering so the centering constants can be
# estimated on the training data only.
house.raw <- HousePrices
index <- sample(1:2, nrow(house.raw), prob = c(.8, .2), replace = TRUE)
house.train <- house.raw[index == 1, ]
house.test <- house.raw[index == 2, ]

# Create centered transformed terms after the split. The training-set centers
# are reused for the test set and the full display data.
house.train <- add_house_features(house.train)
house.centers <- attr(house.train, "feature_centers")
house.test <- add_house_features(house.test, house.centers)
house.data <- add_house_features(house.raw, house.centers)

# Diagnose the expanded candidate set before AIC/CV. This flags collinearity
# but does not remove transformed terms, because AIC/CV should decide whether
# the centered terms improve prediction.
house.collinearity <- diagnose_terms_for_collinearity(
  response_name = "price",
  terms = c(house.base.terms, house.derived.terms, house.interaction.terms),
  data = house.train,
  corr_cutoff = 0.90
)

# Full model: starts with base HousePrices predictors plus all centered
# nonlinear and interaction candidates.
house.model <- lm(house.collinearity$formula, data = house.train)

# Model-selection ladder and repeated CV: the ladder determines the candidate
# models; CV estimates prediction error for each candidate on training folds.
house.ladder <- build_backward_ladder(house.model)
house.cv <- cv_model_ladder(
  data = house.train,
  response_name = "price",
  ladder = house.ladder,
  k = 5,
  repeats = 6,
  seed = 4310
)
house.cv.summary <- summarize_cv_results(house.cv)

# Compare the model AIC likes against the model CV likes. The held-out test set
# is used only after selection to estimate final predictive performance.
house.aic.best.idx <- which.min(house.ladder$steps$aic)
house.cv.best.idx <- which.min(house.cv.summary$RMSE)
house.model.aic.best <- house.ladder$models[[house.aic.best.idx]]
house.model.cv.best <- house.ladder$models[[house.cv.best.idx]]
house.pred.aic.best <- predict(house.model.aic.best, newdata = house.test)
house.pred.cv.best <- predict(house.model.cv.best, newdata = house.test)
house.aic.cv.rmse <- house.cv.summary$RMSE[
  match(house.ladder$steps$step[house.aic.best.idx], house.cv.summary$step)
]
house.compare <- data.frame(
  Model = c("AIC-optimal", "CV-optimal"),
  Step = c(
    house.ladder$steps$step[house.aic.best.idx],
    house.cv.summary$step[house.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  Predictors = c(
    house.ladder$steps$predictors[house.aic.best.idx],
    house.cv.summary$predictors[house.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  CV_RMSE = c(
    house.cv.summary$RMSE[match(house.ladder$steps$step[house.aic.best.idx], house.cv.summary$step)],
    house.cv.summary$RMSE[house.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  Test_RMSE = c(
    rmse(house.pred.aic.best, house.test$price),
    rmse(house.pred.cv.best, house.test$price)
  )
)
house.compare[, 4:5] <- round(house.compare[, 4:5], 4)
house.cv.labels <- paste0("S", house.cv.summary$step_num)

# Cook filtering: first run stepAIC for a practical reduced model and identify
# influential training cases with Cook's distance. After removing those rows,
# rerun model selection before defining the final model.
house.model.red <- stepAIC(house.model, direction = "both", trace = FALSE)
house.cook <- cooks.distance(house.model.red)
house.cutoff <- 4 / nrow(house.train)
house.train.new <- house.train[house.cook < house.cutoff, ]
house.pred.before <- predict(house.model.red, newdata = house.test)

# Repeat the diagnostic, ladder, and CV after Cook filtering. This shows
# whether filtering changes the whole selection process, not just the final
# reduced model.
house.clean.collinearity <- diagnose_terms_for_collinearity(
  response_name = "price",
  terms = c(house.base.terms, house.derived.terms, house.interaction.terms),
  data = house.train.new,
  corr_cutoff = 0.90
)
house.clean.model <- lm(house.clean.collinearity$formula, data = house.train.new)
house.clean.ladder <- build_backward_ladder(house.clean.model)
house.clean.cv <- cv_model_ladder(
  data = house.train.new,
  response_name = "price",
  ladder = house.clean.ladder,
  k = 5,
  repeats = 6,
  seed = 4310
)
house.clean.cv.summary <- summarize_cv_results(house.clean.cv)
house.clean.aic.best.idx <- which.min(house.clean.ladder$steps$aic)
house.clean.cv.best.idx <- which.min(house.clean.cv.summary$RMSE)
house.clean.aic.cv.rmse <- house.clean.cv.summary$RMSE[
  match(house.clean.ladder$steps$step[house.clean.aic.best.idx], house.clean.cv.summary$step)
]
house.clean.cv.labels <- paste0("S", house.clean.cv.summary$step_num)
house.cv.ylim <- range(c(house.cv$RMSE, house.clean.cv$RMSE))
house.clean.model.aic.best <- house.clean.ladder$models[[house.clean.aic.best.idx]]
house.clean.model.cv.best <- house.clean.ladder$models[[house.clean.cv.best.idx]]
house.clean.pred.aic.best <- predict(house.clean.model.aic.best, newdata = house.test)
house.clean.pred.cv.best <- predict(house.clean.model.cv.best, newdata = house.test)

# Final HousePrices model: use the model selected after Cook filtering. If AIC
# and CV agree, the shared post-Cook model is used. If they disagree, use the
# post-Cook CV model because the final goal is prediction on unseen data.
house.post.cook.same.step <- identical(
  house.clean.ladder$steps$step[house.clean.aic.best.idx],
  house.clean.cv.summary$step[house.clean.cv.best.idx]
)
house.clean.final.idx <- if (house.post.cook.same.step) {
  house.clean.aic.best.idx
} else {
  house.clean.cv.best.idx
}
house.model.final <- if (house.post.cook.same.step) {
  house.clean.model.aic.best
} else {
  house.clean.model.cv.best
}
house.final.selection <- if (house.post.cook.same.step) {
  "Post-Cook AIC/CV agreement"
} else {
  "Post-Cook CV-selected model"
}
house.prediction <- predict(house.model.final, newdata = house.test)
house.pred.after <- house.prediction

# Sensitivity table: CV validation results before and after Cook filtering.
house.sensitivity <- data.frame(
  Version = c("Original training data", "Cook-filtered training data"),
  AIC_Best_Step = c(
    house.ladder$steps$step[house.aic.best.idx],
    house.clean.ladder$steps$step[house.clean.aic.best.idx]
  ),
  AIC_CV_RMSE = c(
    house.aic.cv.rmse,
    house.clean.aic.cv.rmse
  ),
  CV_Best_Step = c(
    house.cv.summary$step[house.cv.best.idx],
    house.clean.cv.summary$step[house.clean.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  CV_RMSE = c(
    house.cv.summary$RMSE[house.cv.best.idx],
    house.clean.cv.summary$RMSE[house.clean.cv.best.idx]
  )
)
house.sensitivity[, c("AIC_CV_RMSE", "CV_RMSE")] <-
  round(house.sensitivity[, c("AIC_CV_RMSE", "CV_RMSE")], 2)
house.compare.display <- house.compare[, c("Model", "Step", "Predictors", "CV_RMSE")]
names(house.compare.display) <- c("Model", "Best Step", "Predictors", "CV RMSE")
house.sensitivity.display <- house.sensitivity
names(house.sensitivity.display) <- c(
  "Training Data Used", "AIC Best Step", "AIC RMSE",
  "CV Best Step", "CV RMSE"
)

# Test table: after choosing models on training data, evaluate predictions on
# the untouched 20% test set.
house.test.display <- data.frame(
  Version = c("Original training data", "Cook-filtered training data"),
  AIC_Best_Step = c(
    house.ladder$steps$step[house.aic.best.idx],
    house.clean.ladder$steps$step[house.clean.aic.best.idx]
  ),
  AIC_Test_RMSE = c(
    rmse(house.pred.aic.best, house.test$price),
    rmse(house.clean.pred.aic.best, house.test$price)
  ),
  CV_Best_Step = c(
    house.cv.summary$step[house.cv.best.idx],
    house.clean.cv.summary$step[house.clean.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  CV_Test_RMSE = c(
    rmse(house.pred.cv.best, house.test$price),
    rmse(house.clean.pred.cv.best, house.test$price)
  )
)
house.test.display <- data.frame(
  Version = house.test.display$Version,
  AIC_Best_Step = house.test.display$AIC_Best_Step,
  CV_Best_Step = house.test.display$CV_Best_Step,
  Held_Out_Test_RMSE = c(
    format_test_rmse(
      house.test.display$AIC_Best_Step[1], house.test.display$CV_Best_Step[1],
      house.test.display$AIC_Test_RMSE[1], house.test.display$CV_Test_RMSE[1]
    ),
    format_test_rmse(
      house.test.display$AIC_Best_Step[2], house.test.display$CV_Best_Step[2],
      house.test.display$AIC_Test_RMSE[2], house.test.display$CV_Test_RMSE[2]
    )
  )
)
names(house.test.display) <- c(
  "Training Data Used", "AIC Best Step", "CV Best Step", "Held-out Test RMSE"
)
house.cv.gap <- round(house.compare$CV_RMSE[1] - house.compare$CV_RMSE[2], 2)
house.test.flip <- round(house.compare$Test_RMSE[2] - house.compare$Test_RMSE[1], 2)

# Bootstrap coefficient intervals for the final cleaned model.
house.boot <- bootstrap_coef_table(house.model.final, house.train.new, B = 200)

# Compact final-model summary table.
house.summary <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Selection Used", "Adjusted R2", "Residual s", "Outliers Removed", "Test RMSE", "Test R2"),
  Value = c(
    house.final.selection,
    round(summary(house.model.final)$adj.r.squared, 4),
    round(summary(house.model.final)$sigma, 2),
    length(which(house.cook > house.cutoff)),
    round(rmse(house.prediction, house.test$price), 2),
    round(1 - sum((house.test$price - house.prediction)^2) /
      sum((house.test$price - mean(house.test$price))^2), 4)
  )
)

house.key <- get_term_table(house.model.final)
house.diag <- get_diag(house.model.final)
house.predictors <- paste(attr(terms(house.model.final), "term.labels"), collapse = ", ")
house.removed <- setdiff(attr(terms(house.model), "term.labels"), attr(terms(house.model.final), "term.labels"))
house.removed.text <- if (length(house.removed) == 0) "None from the full model" else paste(house.removed, collapse = ", ")

# Two manually defined prediction scenarios.
house.new <- data.frame(
  lotsize = c(5000, 8000),
  bedrooms = c(3, 4),
  bathrooms = c(1, 2),
  stories = c(1, 2),
  driveway = factor(c("yes", "yes"), levels = levels(house.data$driveway)),
  recreation = factor(c("no", "yes"), levels = levels(house.data$recreation)),
  fullbase = factor(c("no", "yes"), levels = levels(house.data$fullbase)),
  gasheat = factor(c("no", "yes"), levels = levels(house.data$gasheat)),
  aircon = factor(c("no", "yes"), levels = levels(house.data$aircon)),
  garage = c(0, 2),
  prefer = factor(c("no", "yes"), levels = levels(house.data$prefer))
)
house.new <- add_house_features(house.new, house.centers)

house.conf <- predict(house.model.final, newdata = house.new, interval = "confidence")
house.pred <- predict(house.model.final, newdata = house.new, interval = "prediction")

house.pred.table <- data.frame(
  Scenario = c("Smaller home", "Larger home"),
  Fit = round(house.conf[, "fit"], 0),
  CI = paste0("(", round(house.conf[, "lwr"], 0), ", ", round(house.conf[, "upr"], 0), ")"),
  PI = paste0("(", round(house.pred[, "lwr"], 0), ", ", round(house.pred[, "upr"], 0), ")")
)

house.actual.conf <- predict(house.model.final, interval = "confidence")
house.actual.pred <- predict(house.model.final, interval = "prediction")
house.order <- order(house.actual.conf[, "fit"])
house.fit.x <- house.actual.conf[house.order, "fit"]
house.new.fit <- house.conf[, "fit"]

house.process <- data.frame(
  Step = c(
    "Split data",
    "Create centered transformed terms",
    "Diagnose collinearity",
    "Build centered expanded full model",
    "Build model ladder",
    "Run repeated CV",
    "Compare AIC and CV",
    "Remove outliers",
    "Rerun diagnostics and AIC/CV after Cook filtering",
    "Check residuals",
    "Check Box-Cox transformation",
    "Bootstrap intervals",
    "Predict new homes"
  ),
  Detail = c(
    paste(nrow(house.train), "train /", nrow(house.test), "test"),
    paste(length(c(house.derived.terms, house.interaction.terms)), "derived/interaction candidates"),
    paste(nrow(house.collinearity$high_correlations), "high-correlation pairs flagged; 0 pre-AIC removals"),
    paste(length(attr(terms(house.model), "term.labels")), "centered starting predictors"),
    paste(nrow(house.ladder$steps), "nested steps"),
    "5 folds x 6 repeats",
    paste(house.compare$Step[1], "vs", house.compare$Step[2]),
    paste(length(which(house.cook > house.cutoff)), "points above Cook cutoff"),
    paste(
      nrow(house.clean.collinearity$high_correlations),
      "post-Cook high-correlation pairs;",
      house.clean.ladder$steps$step[house.clean.aic.best.idx],
      "AIC and",
      house.clean.cv.summary$step[house.clean.cv.best.idx],
      "CV"
    ),
    "QQ, BP, and DW tests",
    "profile plot and original-scale test metrics",
    "200 bootstrap resamples",
    "2 cases with CI and PI"
  )
)
```

## CASchools Analysis Pipeline

```{r}
#| label: pipeline-schools
#| code-fold: true
#| include: true

data("CASchools", package = "AER")

# â”€â”€ Dataset 2: CASchools analysis pipeline â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
# Goal: predict district test score using class size, demographics, resources,
# income, English learner share, and district grade type.

schools.raw <- CASchools
schools.raw$score <- (schools.raw$read + schools.raw$math) / 2
schools.raw$str <- schools.raw$students / schools.raw$teachers
schools.raw$computer.rate <- schools.raw$computer / schools.raw$students
schools.raw <- schools.raw[, c(
  "score", "str", "calworks", "lunch", "computer.rate",
  "expenditure", "income", "english", "grades"
)]

index <- sample(1:2, nrow(schools.raw), prob = c(.8, .2), replace = TRUE)
schools.train <- schools.raw[index == 1, ]
schools.test <- schools.raw[index == 2, ]
schools.train <- add_school_features(schools.train)
schools.centers <- attr(schools.train, "feature_centers")
schools.test <- add_school_features(schools.test, schools.centers)
schools.data <- add_school_features(schools.raw, schools.centers)

schools.collinearity <- diagnose_terms_for_collinearity(
  response_name = "score",
  terms = c(schools.base.terms, schools.derived.terms, schools.interaction.terms),
  data = schools.train,
  corr_cutoff = 0.90
)

schools.model <- lm(schools.collinearity$formula, data = schools.train)
schools.ladder <- build_backward_ladder(schools.model)

schools.cv <- cv_model_ladder(
  data = schools.train,
  response_name = "score",
  ladder = schools.ladder,
  k = 5,
  repeats = 6,
  seed = 4310
)
schools.cv.summary <- summarize_cv_results(schools.cv)

schools.aic.best.idx <- which.min(schools.ladder$steps$aic)
schools.cv.best.idx <- which.min(schools.cv.summary$RMSE)
schools.model.aic.best <- schools.ladder$models[[schools.aic.best.idx]]
schools.model.cv.best <- schools.ladder$models[[schools.cv.best.idx]]
schools.pred.aic.best <- predict(schools.model.aic.best, newdata = schools.test)
schools.pred.cv.best <- predict(schools.model.cv.best, newdata = schools.test)
schools.aic.cv.rmse <- schools.cv.summary$RMSE[
  match(schools.ladder$steps$step[schools.aic.best.idx], schools.cv.summary$step)
]
schools.compare <- data.frame(
  Model = c("AIC-optimal", "CV-optimal"),
  Step = c(
    schools.ladder$steps$step[schools.aic.best.idx],
    schools.cv.summary$step[schools.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  Predictors = c(
    schools.ladder$steps$predictors[schools.aic.best.idx],
    schools.cv.summary$predictors[schools.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  CV_RMSE = c(
    schools.cv.summary$RMSE[match(schools.ladder$steps$step[schools.aic.best.idx], schools.cv.summary$step)],
    schools.cv.summary$RMSE[schools.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  Test_RMSE = c(
    rmse(schools.pred.aic.best, schools.test$score),
    rmse(schools.pred.cv.best, schools.test$score)
  )
)
schools.compare[, 4:5] <- round(schools.compare[, 4:5], 4)
schools.cv.labels <- paste0("S", schools.cv.summary$step_num)

schools.model.red <- stepAIC(schools.model, direction = "both", trace = FALSE)
schools.cook <- cooks.distance(schools.model.red)
schools.cutoff <- 4 / nrow(schools.train)
schools.train.new <- schools.train[schools.cook < schools.cutoff, ]
schools.pred.before <- predict(schools.model.red, newdata = schools.test)

schools.clean.collinearity <- diagnose_terms_for_collinearity(
  response_name = "score",
  terms = c(schools.base.terms, schools.derived.terms, schools.interaction.terms),
  data = schools.train.new,
  corr_cutoff = 0.90
)
schools.clean.model <- lm(schools.clean.collinearity$formula, data = schools.train.new)
schools.clean.ladder <- build_backward_ladder(schools.clean.model)
schools.clean.cv <- cv_model_ladder(
  data = schools.train.new,
  response_name = "score",
  ladder = schools.clean.ladder,
  k = 5,
  repeats = 6,
  seed = 4310
)
schools.clean.cv.summary <- summarize_cv_results(schools.clean.cv)
schools.clean.aic.best.idx <- which.min(schools.clean.ladder$steps$aic)
schools.clean.cv.best.idx <- which.min(schools.clean.cv.summary$RMSE)
schools.clean.aic.cv.rmse <- schools.clean.cv.summary$RMSE[
  match(schools.clean.ladder$steps$step[schools.clean.aic.best.idx], schools.clean.cv.summary$step)
]
schools.clean.cv.labels <- paste0("S", schools.clean.cv.summary$step_num)
schools.cv.ylim <- range(c(schools.cv$RMSE, schools.clean.cv$RMSE))
schools.clean.model.aic.best <- schools.clean.ladder$models[[schools.clean.aic.best.idx]]
schools.clean.model.cv.best <- schools.clean.ladder$models[[schools.clean.cv.best.idx]]
schools.clean.pred.aic.best <- predict(schools.clean.model.aic.best, newdata = schools.test)
schools.clean.pred.cv.best <- predict(schools.clean.model.cv.best, newdata = schools.test)

schools.post.cook.same.step <- identical(
  schools.clean.ladder$steps$step[schools.clean.aic.best.idx],
  schools.clean.cv.summary$step[schools.clean.cv.best.idx]
)
schools.clean.final.idx <- if (schools.post.cook.same.step) {
  schools.clean.aic.best.idx
} else {
  schools.clean.cv.best.idx
}
schools.model.final <- if (schools.post.cook.same.step) {
  schools.clean.model.aic.best
} else {
  schools.clean.model.cv.best
}
schools.final.selection <- if (schools.post.cook.same.step) {
  "Post-Cook AIC/CV agreement"
} else {
  "Post-Cook CV-selected model"
}
schools.prediction <- predict(schools.model.final, newdata = schools.test)
schools.pred.after <- schools.prediction

schools.sensitivity <- data.frame(
  Version = c("Original training data", "Cook-filtered training data"),
  AIC_Best_Step = c(
    schools.ladder$steps$step[schools.aic.best.idx],
    schools.clean.ladder$steps$step[schools.clean.aic.best.idx]
  ),
  AIC_CV_RMSE = c(
    schools.aic.cv.rmse,
    schools.clean.aic.cv.rmse
  ),
  CV_Best_Step = c(
    schools.cv.summary$step[schools.cv.best.idx],
    schools.clean.cv.summary$step[schools.clean.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  CV_RMSE = c(
    schools.cv.summary$RMSE[schools.cv.best.idx],
    schools.clean.cv.summary$RMSE[schools.clean.cv.best.idx]
  )
)
schools.sensitivity[, c("AIC_CV_RMSE", "CV_RMSE")] <-
  round(schools.sensitivity[, c("AIC_CV_RMSE", "CV_RMSE")], 2)
schools.compare.display <- schools.compare[, c("Model", "Step", "Predictors", "CV_RMSE")]
names(schools.compare.display) <- c("Model", "Best Step", "Predictors", "CV RMSE")
schools.sensitivity.display <- schools.sensitivity
names(schools.sensitivity.display) <- c(
  "Training Data Used", "AIC Best Step", "AIC RMSE",
  "CV Best Step", "CV RMSE"
)

schools.test.display <- data.frame(
  Version = c("Original training data", "Cook-filtered training data"),
  AIC_Best_Step = c(
    schools.ladder$steps$step[schools.aic.best.idx],
    schools.clean.ladder$steps$step[schools.clean.aic.best.idx]
  ),
  AIC_Test_RMSE = c(
    rmse(schools.pred.aic.best, schools.test$score),
    rmse(schools.clean.pred.aic.best, schools.test$score)
  ),
  CV_Best_Step = c(
    schools.cv.summary$step[schools.cv.best.idx],
    schools.clean.cv.summary$step[schools.clean.cv.best.idx]
  ),
  CV_Test_RMSE = c(
    rmse(schools.pred.cv.best, schools.test$score),
    rmse(schools.clean.pred.cv.best, schools.test$score)
  )
)
schools.test.display <- data.frame(
  Version = schools.test.display$Version,
  AIC_Best_Step = schools.test.display$AIC_Best_Step,
  CV_Best_Step = schools.test.display$CV_Best_Step,
  Held_Out_Test_RMSE = c(
    format_test_rmse(
      schools.test.display$AIC_Best_Step[1], schools.test.display$CV_Best_Step[1],
      schools.test.display$AIC_Test_RMSE[1], schools.test.display$CV_Test_RMSE[1]
    ),
    format_test_rmse(
      schools.test.display$AIC_Best_Step[2], schools.test.display$CV_Best_Step[2],
      schools.test.display$AIC_Test_RMSE[2], schools.test.display$CV_Test_RMSE[2]
    )
  )
)
names(schools.test.display) <- c(
  "Training Data Used", "AIC Best Step", "CV Best Step", "Held-out Test RMSE"
)

schools.boot <- bootstrap_coef_table(schools.model.final, schools.train.new, B = 200)

schools.summary <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Selection Used", "Adjusted R2", "Residual s", "Outliers Removed", "Test RMSE", "Test R2"),
  Value = c(
    schools.final.selection,
    round(summary(schools.model.final)$adj.r.squared, 4),
    round(summary(schools.model.final)$sigma, 2),
    length(which(schools.cook > schools.cutoff)),
    round(rmse(schools.prediction, schools.test$score), 2),
    round(1 - sum((schools.test$score - schools.prediction)^2) /
      sum((schools.test$score - mean(schools.test$score))^2), 4)
  )
)

schools.key <- get_term_table(schools.model.final)
schools.diag <- get_diag(schools.model.final)
schools.predictors <- paste(attr(terms(schools.model.final), "term.labels"), collapse = ", ")
schools.removed <- setdiff(attr(terms(schools.model), "term.labels"), attr(terms(schools.model.final), "term.labels"))
schools.removed.text <- if (length(schools.removed) == 0) "None from the full model" else paste(schools.removed, collapse = ", ")

schools.new <- data.frame(
  str = c(18, 22),
  calworks = c(10, 45),
  lunch = c(25, 70),
  computer.rate = c(0.25, 0.12),
  expenditure = c(5500, 5000),
  income = c(20, 12),
  english = c(5, 30),
  grades = factor(c("KK-06", "KK-08"), levels = levels(schools.data$grades))
)
schools.new <- add_school_features(schools.new, schools.centers)

schools.conf <- predict(
  schools.model.final,
  newdata = schools.new,
  interval = "confidence"
)

schools.pred <- predict(
  schools.model.final,
  newdata = schools.new,
  interval = "prediction"
)

schools.pred.table <- data.frame(
  Scenario = c("Lower-risk district", "Higher-risk district"),
  Fit = round(schools.conf[, "fit"], 2),
  CI = paste0("(", round(schools.conf[, "lwr"], 2), ", ", round(schools.conf[, "upr"], 2), ")"),
  PI = paste0("(", round(schools.pred[, "lwr"], 2), ", ", round(schools.pred[, "upr"], 2), ")")
)

schools.actual.conf <- predict(schools.model.final, interval = "confidence")
schools.actual.pred <- predict(schools.model.final, interval = "prediction")
schools.order <- order(schools.actual.conf[, "fit"])
schools.fit.x <- schools.actual.conf[schools.order, "fit"]
schools.new.fit <- schools.conf[, "fit"]

schools.process <- data.frame(
  Step = c(
    "Split data",
    "Create centered transformed terms",
    "Diagnose collinearity",
    "Build centered expanded full model",
    "Build model ladder",
    "Run repeated CV",
    "Compare AIC and CV",
    "Remove outliers",
    "Rerun diagnostics and AIC/CV after Cook filtering",
    "Check residuals",
    "Check Box-Cox transformation",
    "Bootstrap intervals",
    "Predict districts"
  ),
  Detail = c(
    paste(nrow(schools.train), "train /", nrow(schools.test), "test"),
    paste(length(c(schools.derived.terms, schools.interaction.terms)), "derived/interaction candidates"),
    paste(nrow(schools.collinearity$high_correlations), "high-correlation pairs flagged; 0 pre-AIC removals"),
    paste(length(attr(terms(schools.model), "term.labels")), "centered starting predictors"),
    paste(nrow(schools.ladder$steps), "nested steps"),
    "5 folds x 6 repeats",
    paste(schools.compare$Step[1], "vs", schools.compare$Step[2]),
    paste(length(which(schools.cook > schools.cutoff)), "points above Cook cutoff"),
    paste(
      nrow(schools.clean.collinearity$high_correlations),
      "post-Cook high-correlation pairs;",
      schools.clean.ladder$steps$step[schools.clean.aic.best.idx],
      "AIC and",
      schools.clean.cv.summary$step[schools.clean.cv.best.idx],
      "CV"
    ),
    "QQ, BP, and DW tests",
    "profile plot and original-scale test metrics",
    "200 bootstrap resamples",
    "2 cases with CI and PI"
  )
)
```

## Finance Application Pipeline

```{r}
#| label: pipeline-finance
#| code-fold: true
#| include: true

# â”€â”€ Part II: Finance application setup â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
# Goal: use regression-style thinking in finance by comparing individual stocks
# with the S&P 500 market benchmark. The finance section studies return, risk,
# market correlation, CAPM alpha/beta, and rolling market exposure.

finance.stocks <- data.frame(
  Ticker = c("JPM", "JNJ", "XOM", "WMT", "CAT", "BA"),
  Company = c(
    "JPMorgan Chase",
    "Johnson & Johnson",
    "Exxon Mobil",
    "Walmart",
    "Caterpillar",
    "Boeing Company"
  ),
  Industry = c(
    "Financial Services",
    "Healthcare",
    "Energy",
    "Retail & Wholesale",
    "Industrials",
    "Aerospace"
  )
)

JPM.f <- getSymbols("JPM", src = "yahoo", from = "2023-01-01", to = "2025-12-31", auto.assign = FALSE)
JNJ.f <- getSymbols("JNJ", src = "yahoo", from = "2023-01-01", to = "2025-12-31", auto.assign = FALSE)
XOM.f <- getSymbols("XOM", src = "yahoo", from = "2023-01-01", to = "2025-12-31", auto.assign = FALSE)
WMT.f <- getSymbols("WMT", src = "yahoo", from = "2023-01-01", to = "2025-12-31", auto.assign = FALSE)
CAT.f <- getSymbols("CAT", src = "yahoo", from = "2023-01-01", to = "2025-12-31", auto.assign = FALSE)
BA.f <- getSymbols("BA", src = "yahoo", from = "2023-01-01", to = "2025-12-31", auto.assign = FALSE)
GSPC.f <- getSymbols("^GSPC", src = "yahoo", from = "2023-01-01", to = "2025-12-31", auto.assign = FALSE)

finance.close <- na.omit(merge(
  JPM = Cl(JPM.f),
  JNJ = Cl(JNJ.f),
  XOM = Cl(XOM.f),
  WMT = Cl(WMT.f),
  CAT = Cl(CAT.f),
  BA = Cl(BA.f),
  SP500 = Cl(GSPC.f)
))
colnames(finance.close) <- c("JPM", "JNJ", "XOM", "WMT", "CAT", "BA", "SP500")

finance.ret <- na.omit(merge(
  dailyReturn(Cl(JPM.f), type = "arithmetic"),
  dailyReturn(Cl(JNJ.f), type = "arithmetic"),
  dailyReturn(Cl(XOM.f), type = "arithmetic"),
  dailyReturn(Cl(WMT.f), type = "arithmetic"),
  dailyReturn(Cl(CAT.f), type = "arithmetic"),
  dailyReturn(Cl(BA.f), type = "arithmetic"),
  dailyReturn(Cl(GSPC.f), type = "arithmetic")
))
colnames(finance.ret) <- c("JPM", "JNJ", "XOM", "WMT", "CAT","BA", "SP500")
finance.ret.mat <- coredata(finance.ret)

finance.annual <- data.frame(
  Ticker = colnames(finance.ret),
  Annual_Return = round(colMeans(finance.ret.mat) * 252, 4),
  Annual_Risk = round(apply(finance.ret.mat, 2, sd) * sqrt(252), 4)
)
rownames(finance.annual) <- NULL

finance.corr <- round(cor(finance.ret.mat), 3)
finance.cum <- xts(
  apply(1 + finance.ret.mat, 2, cumprod),
  order.by = index(finance.ret)
)

finance.capm <- data.frame(
  Ticker = finance.stocks$Ticker,
  Alpha_Daily = rep(NA, 6),
  Alpha_Annual = rep(NA, 6),
  Beta = rep(NA, 6),
  R_Squared = rep(NA, 6)
)

for (i in 1:6) {
  stock.name <- finance.stocks$Ticker[i]
  model <- lm(finance.ret.mat[, stock.name] ~ finance.ret.mat[, "SP500"])
  finance.capm$Alpha_Daily[i] <- coef(model)[1]
  finance.capm$Alpha_Annual[i] <- coef(model)[1] * 252
  finance.capm$Beta[i] <- coef(model)[2]
  finance.capm$R_Squared[i] <- summary(model)$r.squared
}

finance.capm[, 2:5] <- round(finance.capm[, 2:5], 4)
rownames(finance.capm) <- NULL

finance.compare <- data.frame(
  Ticker = finance.stocks$Ticker,
  Annual_Return = finance.annual$Annual_Return[match(finance.stocks$Ticker, finance.annual$Ticker)],
  Annual_Risk = finance.annual$Annual_Risk[match(finance.stocks$Ticker, finance.annual$Ticker)],
  Corr_SP500 = round(cor(finance.ret.mat[, finance.stocks$Ticker], finance.ret.mat[, "SP500"]), 4)
)
rownames(finance.compare) <- NULL

finance.label.pos <- c(2, 4, 1, 2, 4, 3)
finance.annual.display <- finance.annual
names(finance.annual.display) <- c("Ticker", "Annual Return", "Annual Risk")
finance.compare.display <- finance.compare
names(finance.compare.display) <- c("Ticker", "Annual Return", "Annual Risk", "Corr with SP500")
finance.capm.display <- finance.capm
names(finance.capm.display) <- c("Ticker", "Alpha Daily", "Alpha Annual", "Beta", "R Squared")
finance.top.return <- finance.compare$Ticker[which.max(finance.compare$Annual_Return)]
finance.high.risk <- finance.compare$Ticker[which.max(finance.compare$Annual_Risk)]
finance.low.risk <- finance.compare$Ticker[which.min(finance.compare$Annual_Risk)]
finance.annual.label.pos <- ifelse(finance.annual$Ticker == finance.high.risk, 2, 4)
finance.high.corr <- finance.compare$Ticker[which.max(finance.compare$Corr_SP500)]
finance.low.corr <- finance.compare$Ticker[which.min(finance.compare$Corr_SP500)]
finance.high.beta <- finance.capm$Ticker[which.max(finance.capm$Beta)]
finance.high.rsq <- finance.capm$Ticker[which.max(finance.capm$R_Squared)]
finance.best.cum <- finance.stocks$Ticker[
  which.max(as.numeric(last(finance.cum[, finance.stocks$Ticker])))
]
finance.worst.cum <- finance.stocks$Ticker[
  which.min(as.numeric(last(finance.cum[, finance.stocks$Ticker])))
]

finance.process <- data.frame(
  Step = c(
    "Pull close prices",
    "Convert to daily returns",
    "Annualize return and risk",
    "Compare correlation to market",
    "Plot cumulative returns",
    "Run CAPM regressions",
    "Track rolling beta",
    "Track rolling correlation"
  ),
  Detail = c(
    "Yahoo Finance, 2023-2025",
    "Arithmetic daily returns",
    "Mean*252 and sd*sqrt(252)",
    "Corr(stock, SP500)",
    "Growth of $1 over time",
    "alpha, beta, and R2",
    "60-day beta windows",
    "60-day correlation windows"
  )
)

finance.roll.beta <- na.omit(merge(
  JPM = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "JPM"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "beta"),
  JNJ = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "JNJ"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "beta"),
  XOM = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "XOM"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "beta"),
  WMT = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "WMT"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "beta"),
  CAT = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "CAT"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "beta"),
  BA = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "BA"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "beta")
))

finance.roll.corr <- na.omit(merge(
  JPM = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "JPM"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "correlation"),
  JNJ = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "JNJ"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "correlation"),
  XOM = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "XOM"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "correlation"),
  WMT = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "WMT"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "correlation"),
  CAT = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "CAT"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "correlation"),
  BA = rolling_market_stat(finance.ret[, "BA"], finance.ret[, "SP500"], stat = "correlation")
))
```

# STAT 4310 Regression Project

Two regression dataset analyses (HousePrices, CASchools) followed by a
finance application benchmarking six NYSE stocks against the S&P 500.

## Roadmap

- **Part I** uses two medium-sized datasets with optimal-model workflows.
- **Part II** uses six NYSE stocks from different industries plus the
  S&P 500 benchmark.
- Every section shows methods, interim process steps, diagnostics,
  extra validation, and final interpretation.

## Part I: Regression Methods

```{r}
#| label: tbl-part1-workflow
kable(
  data.frame(
    Step = c(
      "1. Describe data",
      "2. Split Train/Test",
      "3. Create centered transformed terms",
      "4. Diagnose collinearity",
      "5. Build expanded full model",
      "6. Build CV ladder",
      "7. Compare AIC vs CV",
      "8. Remove outliers",
      "9. Rerun AIC/CV after Cook filtering",
      "10. Check residuals",
      "11. Check Box-Cox transformation",
      "12. Bootstrap intervals",
      "13. Predict new cases"
    ),
    Method = c(
      "response, predictors, missingness",
      "80% / 20%",
      "centered nonlinear terms and interactions",
      "correlation/VIF diagnostics only; no pre-AIC removals",
      "lm() with base terms plus all transformed candidates",
      "nested backward models + repeated CV",
      "AIC-optimal vs CV-optimal",
      "Cook's distance",
      "post-Cook diagnostics and model selection",
      "QQ, BP, and DW tests",
      "profile likelihood plus original-scale test metrics",
      "bootstrap coefficient CIs",
      "confidence and prediction intervals"
    )
  )
)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## The workflow spine

Every step from 1 through 9 happens on the **training set only**. The
held-out 20% test set is touched exactly once, at step 13, for honest
out-of-sample error estimation. This ordering is what prevents data
leakage from producing optimistic performance numbers.
:::

# Part I â€” HousePrices Analysis

## Dataset Snapshot

::::{.columns}
:::{.column width="60%"}
- `546` observations and `12` original variables
- Response: `price`
- `lotsize`: lot size of the house
- `bedrooms`, `bathrooms`, `stories`, `garage`: size/layout features
- `driveway`, `recreation`, `fullbase`, `gasheat`, `aircon`, `prefer`:
  yes/no housing amenities or location preference indicators
- Missing values: `0`
- Centered versions, log lot size, squared lot size, and interactions
  are created after the train/test split.
:::

:::{.column width="40%"}
```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-audit
kable(
  data.frame(
    Item = c("Rows", "Columns after engineering", "Missing"),
    Value = c(nrow(house.data), ncol(house.data), sum(is.na(house.data)))
  ),
  row.names = FALSE
)
```
:::
::::

## Exploratory Overview

```{r}
#| label: fig-house-eda
#| fig-width: 9.5
#| fig-height: 4.8
par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4.5, 4, 3, 1))

hist(
  house.data$price,
  breaks = 30,
  col = "#2a6f97",
  border = "white",
  xlab = "Price",
  main = "Distribution of Price"
)

plot(
  house.data$lotsize,
  house.data$price,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#123c55", alpha.f = 0.5),
  xlab = "Lot Size",
  ylab = "Price",
  main = "Price vs Lot Size"
)

plot(
  jitter(house.data$bedrooms),
  house.data$price,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#b83227", alpha.f = 0.5),
  xlab = "Bedrooms",
  ylab = "Price",
  main = "Price vs Bedrooms"
)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## What the EDA tells us

- **Price is right-skewed** â€” a long upper tail of high-value sales that
  could stress the normality assumption later.
- **Lot size shows positive association** with price but wide spread â€”
  lot size alone is insufficient.
- **Bedroom count is a weak single predictor** â€” amenity combinations
  will matter more than any single structural feature.
:::

## Interim Process

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-process
kable(house.process, row.names = FALSE)
```

## VIF and Correlation Diagnostics

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-collinearity
kable(format_screen_summary(house.collinearity, "Initial training data"), row.names = FALSE)
kable(format_screen_summary(house.clean.collinearity, "Cook-filtered training data"), row.names = FALSE)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-vif
kable(format_final_vif_table(house.clean.collinearity, max_rows = 6), row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Why high VIFs are expected here

The expanded candidate set includes squared terms like `lotsize_1000_c_sq`
and interactions like `log_lotsize_c:prefer`. These terms are
mathematically derived from their source variables, so VIF will flag
them â€” that is the design, not a problem. AIC and CV will decide
whether they genuinely improve prediction.
:::

## CV Across the Selection Ladder

```{r}
#| label: fig-house-cv
#| fig-width: 9.2
#| fig-height: 4.7
par(mar = c(7, 4.5, 4, 1))

boxplot(
  RMSE ~ factor(step_num, labels = house.cv.labels),
  data = house.cv,
  las = 2,
  cex.axis = 0.8,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Validation RMSE",
  main = "Repeated 5-fold CV on the training-set model ladder"
)

lines(seq_along(house.cv.labels), house.cv.summary$RMSE, col = "#123c55", lwd = 2)
points(seq_along(house.cv.labels), house.cv.summary$RMSE, pch = 16, col = "#123c55")
points(house.cv.best.idx, house.cv.summary$RMSE[house.cv.best.idx], pch = 19, col = "#0b6e4f", cex = 1.2)

abline(h = house.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#b83227", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Mean CV RMSE", "CV-best step", "AIC-selected step mean CV RMSE"),
  col = c("#123c55", "#0b6e4f", "#b83227"),
  pch = c(16, 19, NA),
  lty = c(1, NA, 2),
  lwd = c(2, NA, 2),
  bty = "n"
)

par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Reading the CV ladder

`S0` is the full model; each later step removes one more predictor.
Each box represents **30 validation RMSE values** (5 folds Ă— 6 repeats).
The green point marks the true CV minimum; the red dashed line marks
the AIC-selected step's mean CV RMSE. One early predictor removal
improves validation RMSE, but further trimming gives up real signal.
:::

## AIC Step vs CV Step

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-aic-cv
kable(house.compare.display, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Decision

For `HousePrices`, the CV gap between AIC-optimal and CV-optimal is only
`r house.cv.gap`. This is small relative to the scale of house prices,
so the test-set flip of `r house.test.flip` is one-split noise rather
than a genuine criterion contradiction. **We proceed with the
CV-selected model.**
:::

## Sensitivity to Cook Filtering

::: {.callout-warning}
## Why this matters

A single weird sale â€” say, a mansion sold for $1 â€” can drag the
least-squares fit so much that predictions for every other house
suffer. **Cook's distance** measures how much each observation shifts
the fit. Rows where $D_i > 4/n$ are flagged as influential. We then ask
two questions: do these observations change *which* model we pick, or
just *how accurate* it is?
:::

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-sensitivity
kable(house.sensitivity.display, row.names = FALSE)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-test-sensitivity
kable(house.test.display, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Interpretation

- Upper table: both RMSE columns are **repeated-CV validation** RMSE
  from the training data.
- Lower table: RMSE on the **held-out 20% test set**.
- When AIC and CV pick the same step, that test RMSE is shown once.
- After Cook filtering, validation error drops without changing the
  overall selection story â€” the model choice is robust.
:::

## CV Ladder Before and After Cook Filtering

```{r}
#| label: fig-house-cv-cook
#| fig-width: 9.2
#| fig-height: 4.6
par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(7, 4.2, 3.5, 1))

boxplot(
  RMSE ~ factor(step_num, labels = house.cv.labels),
  data = house.cv,
  las = 2,
  cex.axis = 0.8,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Validation RMSE",
  ylim = house.cv.ylim,
  main = "Before Cook filtering"
)

lines(seq_len(nrow(house.cv.summary)), house.cv.summary$RMSE, col = "#123c55", lwd = 2)
points(seq_len(nrow(house.cv.summary)), house.cv.summary$RMSE, pch = 16, col = "#123c55")
abline(h = house.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#b83227", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Mean CV RMSE", "AIC-selected step"),
  col = c("#123c55", "#b83227"),
  pch = c(16, NA),
  lty = c(1, 2),
  lwd = c(2, 2),
  bty = "n",
  cex = 0.8
)

plot(
  house.cv.summary$step_num,
  house.cv.summary$RMSE,
  type = "b",
  pch = 16,
  lwd = 2,
  col = "gray40",
  xaxt = "n",
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Mean validation RMSE",
  ylim = house.cv.ylim,
  main = "Mean CV RMSE overlay"
)

lines(
  house.clean.cv.summary$step_num,
  house.clean.cv.summary$RMSE,
  type = "b",
  pch = 17,
  lwd = 2,
  col = "#0b6e4f"
)

abline(h = house.aic.cv.rmse, col = "gray40", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
abline(h = house.clean.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2, lwd = 2)

axis(1, at = house.cv.summary$step_num, labels = house.cv.labels, las = 2, cex.axis = 0.8)

legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Before Cook", "After Cook", "Before AIC RMSE", "After AIC RMSE"),
  col = c("gray40", "#0b6e4f", "gray40", "#0b6e4f"),
  pch = c(16, 17, NA, NA),
  lty = c(1, 1, 2, 2),
  lwd = c(2, 2, 2, 2),
  bty = "n",
  cex = 0.8
)

boxplot(
  RMSE ~ factor(step_num, labels = house.clean.cv.labels),
  data = house.clean.cv,
  las = 2,
  cex.axis = 0.8,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Validation RMSE",
  ylim = house.cv.ylim,
  main = "After Cook filtering"
)

lines(seq_len(nrow(house.clean.cv.summary)), house.clean.cv.summary$RMSE, col = "#123c55", lwd = 2)
points(seq_len(nrow(house.clean.cv.summary)), house.clean.cv.summary$RMSE, pch = 16, col = "#123c55")
abline(h = house.clean.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#b83227", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Mean CV RMSE", "AIC-selected step"),
  col = c("#123c55", "#b83227"),
  pch = c(16, NA),
  lty = c(1, 2),
  lwd = c(2, 2),
  bty = "n",
  cex = 0.8
)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

::: {.callout-important}
## The whole-ladder shift

The entire CV curve drops after Cook filtering â€” **not just the
selected step**. This rules out the suspicion that outlier removal is
overfitting to one lucky model. Cook filtering here produces genuine
stability improvement across every candidate model on the ladder.
:::

## Final Model Summary

- **Final predictors kept:** `r house.predictors`
- **Removed from full model:** `r house.removed.text`

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-summary
kable(house.summary, row.names = FALSE)
```

## Final Model Terms

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-terms
kable(house.key, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Coefficient interpretation

Each coefficient is a **partial effect** â€” the effect of that predictor
holding all others fixed. Larger lots, more stories and bathrooms, air
conditioning, a garage, and preferred location all move fitted price
upward. Bedrooms can appear non-significant here even though EDA
showed a positive trend â€” that's because correlated features (like
bathrooms and stories) now absorb its explanatory power.
:::

## Transformed Terms Kept

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-engineered
house.engineered.pool <- c(house.derived.terms, house.interaction.terms)
house.final.terms <- get_model_terms(house.model.final)
house.selected.derived <- intersect(house.derived.terms, house.final.terms)
house.selected.interactions <- intersect(house.interaction.terms, house.final.terms)

house.engineered.status <- data.frame(
  Term = house.engineered.pool,
  Type = c(
    rep("Derived", length(house.derived.terms)),
    rep("Interaction", length(house.interaction.terms))
  ),
  Meaning = c(
    "Centered natural log of lot size.",
    "Squared centered lot size in thousands.",
    "Centered bedrooms multiplied by centered bathrooms.",
    "Centered bathrooms multiplied by centered stories.",
    "Centered garage spaces multiplied by preferred-neighborhood indicator.",
    "Centered log lot size slope allowed to differ by preferred-neighborhood status.",
    "Centered log lot size slope allowed to differ by air-conditioning status.",
    "Centered garage-space effect allowed to vary by centered lot size."
  ),
  `Kept In Final Model` = ifelse(house.engineered.pool %in% house.final.terms, "Yes", "No"),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(house.engineered.status, row.names = FALSE)
```

## Diagnostics

```{r}
#| label: fig-house-diag
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4.2
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
qqnorm(house.model.final$residuals, main = "QQ Plot")
qqline(house.model.final$residuals)
plot(
  house.model.final$fitted.values,
  house.model.final$residuals,
  pch = 16,
  xlab = "Fitted",
  ylab = "Residuals",
  main = "Residuals vs Fitted"
)
abline(h = 0, lty = "dashed")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-diag
kable(house.diag, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## Mild assumption violations

Shapiro-Wilk and Breusch-Pagan may report small p-values for residual
non-normality and heteroscedasticity. In housing data this is common â€”
luxury homes have more idiosyncratic pricing variation than modest
ones. The Durbin-Watson result is an artifact of spatial clustering
in cross-sectional data, not true time-series autocorrelation. With
$n = 448$, the Central Limit Theorem protects coefficient inference,
and bootstrap intervals (shown below) provide assumption-free backup.
:::

## Box-Cox Response Check

```{r}
#| label: fig-house-boxcox
#| fig-width: 7.5
#| fig-height: 4.2
house.boxcox <- boxcox_fit_and_evaluate(
  house.model.final,
  house.train.new,
  house.test,
  "price",
  "HousePrices"
)

plot_boxcox_profile(house.boxcox$profile, "HousePrices Box-Cox profile")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-boxcox-decision
kable(boxcox_decision_table(
  house.boxcox,
  ifelse(
    house.boxcox$profile$identity.in.interval,
    "Keep original price scale; lambda = 1 remains plausible.",
    "Report as sensitivity evidence; lambda = 1 is outside the profile interval."
  )
), row.names = FALSE)
```

The profile peaks near lambda `r round(house.boxcox$profile$lambda, 2)`;
lambda = 1 is `r ifelse(house.boxcox$profile$identity.in.interval, "inside", "outside")`
the approximate 95% interval.

## Box-Cox Test Metrics

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-boxcox-metrics
kable(boxcox_performance_table(house.boxcox), row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why we keep the original scale

Box-Cox is treated as an **assumption sensitivity check**, not a
default replacement for the original response. The RMSE comparison is
on the original dollar scale so the transformed-response model is not
rewarded just for changing units. The smearing back-transform corrects
for Jensen's inequality bias.
:::

## Bootstrap Coefficient Intervals

```{r}
#| label: fig-house-bootstrap
#| fig-width: 9.2
#| fig-height: 5.2
house.boot.plot <- subset(house.boot, term != "(Intercept)")
house.boot.plot <- house.boot.plot[nrow(house.boot.plot):1, ]

plot(
  house.boot.plot$estimate,
  seq_len(nrow(house.boot.plot)),
  xlim = range(c(house.boot.plot$lower, house.boot.plot$upper)),
  pch = 16,
  yaxt = "n",
  ylab = "",
  xlab = "Coefficient estimate",
  main = "Bootstrap 95% intervals for final-model coefficients"
)

segments(
  house.boot.plot$lower, seq_len(nrow(house.boot.plot)),
  house.boot.plot$upper, seq_len(nrow(house.boot.plot)),
  lwd = 2,
  col = "#0b6e4f"
)

abline(v = 0, lty = "dashed", col = "gray50")
axis(2, at = seq_len(nrow(house.boot.plot)), labels = house.boot.plot$term, las = 2)
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Reading bootstrap intervals

Intervals entirely to the right of zero indicate predictors with
**stable positive coefficients** across 200 resamples. Intervals that
overlap zero are consistent with non-significant $t$-test p-values.
The intercept is omitted because its much larger scale would compress
the predictor effects visually.
:::

## Prediction Examples

```{r}
#| label: tbl-house-predictions
kable(house.pred.table, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-important}
## CI versus PI â€” the distinction that matters

- The **confidence interval (CI)** estimates the *mean* price for homes
  with that feature combination.
- The **prediction interval (PI)** estimates an *individual home sale*
  and is always wider because it accounts for irreducible property-level
  variation (negotiation, condition, buyer preferences).
