The higher-amenity scenario gets the higher fitted price, which matches
the coefficient signs in the final model.
:::

## CI and PI Overlay

```{r}
#| label: fig-house-cipi
#| fig-width: 9.5
#| fig-height: 4.7
plot(
  house.fit.x,
  house.train.new$price[house.order],
  pch = 16,
  col = "gray40",
  xlab = "Fitted mean response (ordered low to high)",
  ylab = "Price",
  main = "Actual data with fitted CI/PI and new prediction intervals"
)

lines(house.fit.x, house.fit.x, col = "#0b6e4f", lwd = 2)
lines(house.fit.x, house.actual.conf[house.order, "lwr"], col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2)
lines(house.fit.x, house.actual.conf[house.order, "upr"], col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2)
lines(house.fit.x, house.actual.pred[house.order, "lwr"], col = "#c97c10", lty = 3)
lines(house.fit.x, house.actual.pred[house.order, "upr"], col = "#c97c10", lty = 3)

points(house.new.fit, house.new.fit, pch = 17, col = "#b83227", cex = 1.3)
segments(house.new.fit, house.pred[, "lwr"], house.new.fit, house.pred[, "upr"],
         col = "#f0b44c", lwd = 2)
segments(house.new.fit, house.conf[, "lwr"], house.new.fit, house.conf[, "upr"],
         col = "#b83227", lwd = 3)
```

## Predicted vs Actual (Test Set)

```{r}
#| label: fig-house-test
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5.0
plot(
  house.prediction,
  house.test$price,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#2a6f97", alpha.f = 0.6),
  xlab = "Predicted Price",
  ylab = "Actual Price",
  main = "Predicted vs Actual â€” held-out test set"
)
abline(0, 1, col = "#b83227", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = "Perfect prediction line (slope = 1)",
  col = "#b83227",
  lty = 2,
  lwd = 2,
  bty = "n"
)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## The moment of truth

This is the held-out 20% test set â€” the model has never seen these
homes. Points close to the dashed line indicate accurate predictions.
Deviations are largest at high prices because luxury properties have
more unique features that no model can fully capture.
:::

# Part I â€” CASchools Analysis

## Dataset Snapshot

::::{.columns}
:::{.column width="60%"}
- `420` observations and `9` original analysis variables
- Response: average test score `score`
- `str`: students per teacher
- `calworks`: percent of students on CalWORKs (welfare)
- `lunch`: percent eligible for subsidized lunch
- `income`: district income level
- `english`: percent of English learners
- `grades`: district grade-span (K-6 vs K-8)
- Missing values: `0`
:::

:::{.column width="40%"}
```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-audit
kable(
  data.frame(
    Item = c("Rows", "Columns after engineering", "Missing"),
    Value = c(nrow(schools.data), ncol(schools.data), sum(is.na(schools.data)))
  ),
  row.names = FALSE
)
```
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
## Analytical question

The predictors mix **school-resource variables** (student-teacher ratio,
per-pupil expenditure) with **student-composition variables** (lunch
rate, English learner share, district income). The central question is
which group dominates district-level score variation.
:::

## Exploratory Overview

```{r}
#| label: fig-schools-eda
#| fig-width: 9.5
#| fig-height: 4.8
par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4.5, 4, 3, 1))

hist(
  schools.data$score,
  breaks = 25,
  col = "#2a6f97",
  border = "white",
  xlab = "Score",
  main = "Distribution of Score"
)

plot(
  schools.data$lunch,
  schools.data$score,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#0b6e4f", alpha.f = 0.5),
  xlab = "% Subsidized Lunch",
  ylab = "Score",
  main = "Score vs Lunch"
)

plot(
  schools.data$income,
  schools.data$score,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#b83227", alpha.f = 0.5),
  xlab = "District Income",
  ylab = "Score",
  main = "Score vs Income"
)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Contrast with HousePrices

Score is **roughly symmetric and bell-shaped** â€” no right-skew problem
like HousePrices. The strong negative relationship with lunch rate and
positive association with income are the dominant raw signals. Student
composition already looks more predictive than any instructional-resource
variable.
:::

## Interim Process

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-process
kable(schools.process, row.names = FALSE)
```

## VIF and Correlation Diagnostics

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-collinearity
kable(format_screen_summary(schools.collinearity, "Initial training data"), row.names = FALSE)
kable(format_screen_summary(schools.clean.collinearity, "Cook-filtered training data"), row.names = FALSE)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-vif
kable(format_final_vif_table(schools.clean.collinearity, max_rows = 6), row.names = FALSE)
```

## CV Across the Selection Ladder

```{r}
#| label: fig-schools-cv
#| fig-width: 9.2
#| fig-height: 4.7
par(mar = c(7, 4.5, 4, 1))

boxplot(
  RMSE ~ factor(step_num, labels = schools.cv.labels),
  data = schools.cv,
  las = 2,
  cex.axis = 0.8,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Validation RMSE",
  main = "Repeated 5-fold CV on the training-set model ladder"
)

lines(seq_along(schools.cv.labels), schools.cv.summary$RMSE, col = "#123c55", lwd = 2)
points(seq_along(schools.cv.labels), schools.cv.summary$RMSE, pch = 16, col = "#123c55")
points(schools.cv.best.idx, schools.cv.summary$RMSE[schools.cv.best.idx], pch = 19, col = "#0b6e4f", cex = 1.2)

abline(h = schools.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#b83227", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Mean CV RMSE", "CV-best step", "AIC-selected step mean CV RMSE"),
  col = c("#123c55", "#0b6e4f", "#b83227"),
  pch = c(16, 19, NA),
  lty = c(1, NA, 2),
  lwd = c(2, NA, 2),
  bty = "n"
)

par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

## AIC Step vs CV Step

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-aic-cv
kable(schools.compare.display, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-important}
## AIC and CV agreement

Both criteria select the same reduced model. When AIC and CV agree on
the same step, the reduction is **not an artifact of one criterion** â€”
it's a genuine structural signal in the data. This makes the CASchools
selection argument much stronger than the HousePrices one.
:::

## Sensitivity to Cook Filtering

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-sensitivity
kable(schools.sensitivity.display, row.names = FALSE)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-test-sensitivity
kable(schools.test.display, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Stability story

The selected step does not move after filtering. The influential
districts were not driving model choice â€” just inflating residual
variance. The main school result is robust to a handful of extreme
districts.
:::

## CV Ladder Before and After Cook Filtering

```{r}
#| label: fig-schools-cv-cook
#| fig-width: 9.2
#| fig-height: 4.6
par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(7, 4.2, 3.5, 1))

boxplot(
  RMSE ~ factor(step_num, labels = schools.cv.labels),
  data = schools.cv,
  las = 2,
  cex.axis = 0.8,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Validation RMSE",
  ylim = schools.cv.ylim,
  main = "Before Cook filtering"
)
lines(seq_len(nrow(schools.cv.summary)), schools.cv.summary$RMSE, col = "#123c55", lwd = 2)
points(seq_len(nrow(schools.cv.summary)), schools.cv.summary$RMSE, pch = 16, col = "#123c55")
abline(h = schools.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#b83227", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Mean CV RMSE", "AIC-selected step"),
  col = c("#123c55", "#b83227"),
  pch = c(16, NA),
  lty = c(1, 2),
  lwd = c(2, 2),
  bty = "n",
  cex = 0.8
)

plot(
  schools.cv.summary$step_num,
  schools.cv.summary$RMSE,
  type = "b",
  pch = 16,
  lwd = 2,
  col = "gray40",
  xaxt = "n",
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Mean validation RMSE",
  ylim = schools.cv.ylim,
  main = "Mean CV RMSE overlay"
)
lines(
  schools.clean.cv.summary$step_num,
  schools.clean.cv.summary$RMSE,
  type = "b",
  pch = 17,
  lwd = 2,
  col = "#0b6e4f"
)
abline(h = schools.aic.cv.rmse, col = "gray40", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
abline(h = schools.clean.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
axis(1, at = schools.cv.summary$step_num, labels = schools.cv.labels, las = 2, cex.axis = 0.8)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Before Cook", "After Cook", "Before AIC RMSE", "After AIC RMSE"),
  col = c("gray40", "#0b6e4f", "gray40", "#0b6e4f"),
  pch = c(16, 17, NA, NA),
  lty = c(1, 1, 2, 2),
  lwd = c(2, 2, 2, 2),
  bty = "n",
  cex = 0.8
)

boxplot(
  RMSE ~ factor(step_num, labels = schools.clean.cv.labels),
  data = schools.clean.cv,
  las = 2,
  cex.axis = 0.8,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Validation RMSE",
  ylim = schools.cv.ylim,
  main = "After Cook filtering"
)
lines(seq_len(nrow(schools.clean.cv.summary)), schools.clean.cv.summary$RMSE, col = "#123c55", lwd = 2)
points(seq_len(nrow(schools.clean.cv.summary)), schools.clean.cv.summary$RMSE, pch = 16, col = "#123c55")
abline(h = schools.clean.aic.cv.rmse, col = "#b83227", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Mean CV RMSE", "AIC-selected step"),
  col = c("#123c55", "#b83227"),
  pch = c(16, NA),
  lty = c(1, 2),
  lwd = c(2, 2),
  bty = "n",
  cex = 0.8
)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

## Final Model

- **Final predictors kept:** `r schools.predictors`
- **Removed from full model:** `r schools.removed.text`

::::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-summary
kable(schools.summary, row.names = FALSE)
```
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-terms
kable(schools.key, row.names = FALSE)
```
:::
::::

::: {.callout-important}
## The answer to the analytical question

Once poverty proxies (`lunch`) and district income are in the model,
extra resource variables add much less explanatory power. `calworks`
was dropped because its information is largely captured by `lunch`.
**Student composition dominates instructional resources** in explaining
district-level test score variation.
:::

## Transformed Terms Kept

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-engineered
schools.engineered.pool <- c(schools.derived.terms, schools.interaction.terms)
schools.final.terms <- get_model_terms(schools.model.final)
schools.selected.derived <- intersect(schools.derived.terms, schools.final.terms)
schools.selected.interactions <- intersect(schools.interaction.terms, schools.final.terms)

schools.engineered.status <- data.frame(
  Term = schools.engineered.pool,
  Type = c(
    rep("Derived", length(schools.derived.terms)),
    rep("Interaction", length(schools.interaction.terms))
  ),
  Meaning = c(
    "Squared centered district income.",
    "Squared centered subsidized-lunch percentage.",
    "Squared centered English-learner percentage.",
    "Centered lunch percentage multiplied by centered English-learner percentage.",
    "Centered income multiplied by centered lunch percentage.",
    "Centered student-teacher ratio multiplied by centered lunch percentage.",
    "Centered lunch slope allowed to differ by district grade span.",
    "Centered income slope allowed to differ by district grade span.",
    "Centered English-learner slope allowed to differ by district grade span.",
    "Centered student-teacher-ratio slope allowed to differ by district grade span."
  ),
  `Kept In Final Model` = ifelse(schools.engineered.pool %in% schools.final.terms, "Yes", "No"),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(schools.engineered.status, row.names = FALSE)
```

## Diagnostics

```{r}
#| label: fig-schools-diag
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4.2
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
qqnorm(schools.model.final$residuals, main = "QQ Plot")
qqline(schools.model.final$residuals)
plot(
  schools.model.final$fitted.values,
  schools.model.final$residuals,
  pch = 16,
  xlab = "Fitted",
  ylab = "Residuals",
  main = "Residuals vs Fitted"
)
abline(h = 0, lty = "dashed")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-diag
kable(schools.diag, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Cleaner diagnostics than HousePrices

The Shapiro-Wilk p-value here is typically well above 0.05 â€” residuals
behave normally, a consequence of the more symmetric score distribution
we saw in EDA. The CASchools model passes its diagnostic checks more
cleanly than HousePrices did.
:::

## Box-Cox Response Check

```{r}
#| label: fig-schools-boxcox
#| fig-width: 7.5
#| fig-height: 4.2
schools.boxcox <- boxcox_fit_and_evaluate(
  schools.model.final,
  schools.train.new,
  schools.test,
  "score",
  "CASchools"
)

plot_boxcox_profile(schools.boxcox$profile, "CASchools Box-Cox profile")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-boxcox-decision
kable(boxcox_decision_table(
  schools.boxcox,
  ifelse(
    schools.boxcox$profile$identity.in.interval,
    "Keep original score scale; lambda = 1 remains plausible.",
    "Use/report Box-Cox as sensitivity evidence; lambda = 1 is outside the profile interval."
  )
), row.names = FALSE)
```

The profile peaks near lambda `r round(schools.boxcox$profile$lambda, 2)`;
lambda = 1 is `r ifelse(schools.boxcox$profile$identity.in.interval, "inside", "outside")`
the approximate 95% interval.

## Box-Cox Test Metrics

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-boxcox-metrics
kable(boxcox_performance_table(schools.boxcox), row.names = FALSE)
```

## Bootstrap Coefficient Intervals

```{r}
#| label: fig-schools-bootstrap
#| fig-width: 9.2
#| fig-height: 5.2
schools.boot.plot <- subset(schools.boot, term != "(Intercept)")
schools.boot.plot <- schools.boot.plot[nrow(schools.boot.plot):1, ]

plot(
  schools.boot.plot$estimate,
  seq_len(nrow(schools.boot.plot)),
  xlim = range(c(schools.boot.plot$lower, schools.boot.plot$upper)),
  pch = 16,
  yaxt = "n",
  ylab = "",
  xlab = "Coefficient estimate",
  main = "Bootstrap 95% intervals for final-model coefficients"
)

segments(
  schools.boot.plot$lower, seq_len(nrow(schools.boot.plot)),
  schools.boot.plot$upper, seq_len(nrow(schools.boot.plot)),
  lwd = 2,
  col = "#0b6e4f"
)

abline(v = 0, lty = "dashed", col = "gray50")
axis(2, at = seq_len(nrow(schools.boot.plot)), labels = schools.boot.plot$term, las = 2)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Stable socioeconomic effects

`lunch` and `income` remain the most stable effects, sitting far from
zero across all 200 bootstrap resamples. Weaker terms cluster closer
to zero and deserve more caution in interpretation.
:::

## Prediction Examples

```{r}
#| label: tbl-schools-predictions
kable(schools.pred.table, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Tight CIs, wider PIs

The CIs here are remarkably narrow, reflecting the high adjusted
$R^2$ of the model. The PIs are about 14 score points wide,
representing the irreducible district-to-district variability that
even a good model cannot eliminate.
:::

## CI and PI Overlay

```{r}
#| label: fig-schools-cipi
#| fig-width: 9.5
#| fig-height: 4.7
plot(
  schools.fit.x,
  schools.train.new$score[schools.order],
  pch = 16,
  col = "gray40",
  xlab = "Fitted mean response (ordered low to high)",
  ylab = "Score",
  main = "Actual data with fitted CI/PI and new prediction intervals"
)

lines(schools.fit.x, schools.fit.x, col = "#0b6e4f", lwd = 2)
lines(schools.fit.x, schools.actual.conf[schools.order, "lwr"], col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2)
lines(schools.fit.x, schools.actual.conf[schools.order, "upr"], col = "#0b6e4f", lty = 2)
lines(schools.fit.x, schools.actual.pred[schools.order, "lwr"], col = "#c97c10", lty = 3)
lines(schools.fit.x, schools.actual.pred[schools.order, "upr"], col = "#c97c10", lty = 3)

points(schools.new.fit, schools.new.fit, pch = 17, col = "#b83227", cex = 1.3)
segments(schools.new.fit, schools.pred[, "lwr"], schools.new.fit, schools.pred[, "upr"],
         col = "#f0b44c", lwd = 2)
segments(schools.new.fit, schools.conf[, "lwr"], schools.new.fit, schools.conf[, "upr"],
         col = "#b83227", lwd = 3)
```

## Predicted vs Actual (Test Set)

```{r}
#| label: fig-schools-test
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5.0
plot(
  schools.prediction,
  schools.test$score,
  pch = 16,
  col = adjustcolor("#0b6e4f", alpha.f = 0.6),
  xlab = "Predicted Score",
  ylab = "Actual Score",
  main = "Predicted vs Actual â€” held-out test set"
)
abline(0, 1, col = "#b83227", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = "Perfect prediction line (slope = 1)",
  col = "#b83227",
  lty = 2,
  lwd = 2,
  bty = "n"
)
```

# Part II â€” Finance Application

Three years of daily close prices (2023 through 2025) for six NYSE-listed
stocks, benchmarked against the S&P 500 index.

## Method Steps

```{r}
#| label: tbl-finance-process
kable(finance.process, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Why returns, not prices

Stocks trade at wildly different absolute prices â€” a $200 stock and a
$20 stock are not directly comparable. **Returns are percentage changes**,
which normalize across price levels and let us compare risk, co-movement,
and CAPM exposure on equal footing. Annualization multiplies mean daily
return by 252 trading days and scales volatility by $\sqrt{252}$.
:::

## Stock Selection

```{r}
#| label: tbl-finance-stocks
kable(finance.stocks, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why sector diversity matters

The six stocks span financials, healthcare, energy, retail, industrials,
and aerospace. This intentional diversity means we expect **visibly
different betas, correlations, and return profiles** â€” the analysis
becomes more informative than if we had picked six similar firms. The
S&P 500 (`^GSPC`) is the market benchmark.
:::

## Daily Return Distributions

```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-returns
#| fig-width: 9.5
#| fig-height: 5.0
par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(4, 3.5, 3, 1))

for (ticker in finance.stocks$Ticker) {
  hist(
    finance.ret.mat[, ticker],
    breaks = 40,
    col = "#2a6f97",
    border = "white",
    xlab = "Daily Return",
    main = ticker,
    cex.main = 1
  )
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## Fat tails are real

All six stocks show **fat-tailed return distributions** â€” extreme days
occur more often than a normal distribution predicts (excess kurtosis).
This is a well-known empirical property of equity returns and justifies
using bootstrap methods rather than normal-theory intervals for
coefficient inference.
:::

## Close Prices

```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-close
#| fig-width: 9.5
#| fig-height: 5.2
par(mfrow = c(3, 2))

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "JPM"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "JPM")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "JNJ"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "JNJ")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "XOM"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "XOM")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "WMT"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "WMT")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "CAT"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "CAT")

plot(index(finance.close), as.numeric(finance.close[, "BA"]),
     type = "l", xlab = "Date", ylab = "Close", main = "BA")

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

## Annualized Return and Risk

```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-riskreturn
#| fig-width: 8.8
#| fig-height: 5.0
plot(
  finance.annual$Annual_Risk,
  finance.annual$Annual_Return,
  xlim = range(finance.annual$Annual_Risk) + c(-0.01, 0.02),
  ylim = range(finance.annual$Annual_Return) + c(-0.02, 0.03),
  pch = 21,
  bg = "#2a6f97",
  col = "#16384f",
  cex = 1.4,
  xlab = "Annualized Risk",
  ylab = "Annual Return",
  main = "Return-Risk"
)
text(
  finance.annual$Annual_Risk,
  finance.annual$Annual_Return,
  labels = finance.annual$Ticker,
  pos = finance.annual.label.pos,
  offset = 0.55,
  cex = 0.9,
  xpd = NA
)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Reading the risk-return plot

`r finance.top.return` has the strongest annualized return, while
`r finance.high.risk` carries the most risk. `r finance.low.risk` sits
at the defensive end. The classic risk-return tradeoff is visible â€”
higher expected return tends to come with higher volatility, but the
relationship is not perfectly monotonic.
:::

## Correlation with S&P 500 vs Risk and Return

::::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| label: tbl-finance-compare
kable(finance.compare.display, row.names = FALSE)
```
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-bubble
#| fig-width: 4.8
#| fig-height: 4.4
plot(
  finance.compare$Corr_SP500,
  finance.compare$Annual_Return,
  type = "n",
  xlim = range(finance.compare$Corr_SP500) + c(-0.08, 0.08),
  ylim = range(finance.compare$Annual_Return) + c(-0.03, 0.05),
  xlab = "Corr with SP500",
  ylab = "Annual Return",
  main = "Market correlation vs return"
)

symbols(
  finance.compare$Corr_SP500,
  finance.compare$Annual_Return,
  circles = finance.compare$Annual_Risk,
  inches = 0.2,
  add = TRUE,
  bg = adjustcolor("#2a6f97", alpha.f = 0.25),
  fg = "#2a6f97"
)

text(
  finance.compare$Corr_SP500,
  finance.compare$Annual_Return,
  labels = finance.compare$Ticker,
  pos = finance.label.pos,
  offset = 0.8,
  cex = 0.8,
  xpd = NA
)
```
:::
::::

## Linking Risk-Return and Market Correlation

```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-link
#| fig-width: 9.0
#| fig-height: 5.0
risk.x <- 0.8 + (finance.compare$Annual_Risk - min(finance.compare$Annual_Risk)) /
  diff(range(finance.compare$Annual_Risk)) * 1.0
corr.x <- 3.2 + (finance.compare$Corr_SP500 - min(finance.compare$Corr_SP500)) /
  diff(range(finance.compare$Corr_SP500)) * 1.0
ylims <- range(finance.compare$Annual_Return) + c(-0.03, 0.05)

plot(
  NA,
  xlim = c(0.4, 4.6),
  ylim = ylims,
  xaxt = "n",
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "Annual Return",
  main = "Risk-return points linked to market-correlation bubbles"
)

rect(0.55, ylims[1], 2.0, ylims[2], border = "#d9cdb7", col = adjustcolor("#f7efe1", alpha.f = 0.55))
rect(3.0, ylims[1], 4.45, ylims[2], border = "#d9cdb7", col = adjustcolor("#eef6f4", alpha.f = 0.65))
axis(1, at = c(1.275, 3.6), labels = c("Annualized Risk", "Corr with SP500"))

for (i in 1:nrow(finance.compare)) {
  segments(risk.x[i], finance.compare$Annual_Return[i], corr.x[i], finance.compare$Annual_Return[i],
           lty = 3, lwd = 1.5, col = adjustcolor("#7a8793", alpha.f = 0.8))
}

points(risk.x, finance.compare$Annual_Return, pch = 16, col = "#16384f", cex = 1.2)
symbols(
  corr.x,
  finance.compare$Annual_Return,
  circles = finance.compare$Annual_Risk,
  inches = 0.12,
  add = TRUE,
  bg = adjustcolor("#2a6f97", alpha.f = 0.25),
  fg = "#2a6f97"
)

text(risk.x, finance.compare$Annual_Return, labels = finance.compare$Ticker, pos = 2, offset = 0.6, cex = 0.8)
text(corr.x, finance.compare$Annual_Return, labels = finance.compare$Ticker, pos = 4, offset = 0.6, cex = 0.8)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Volatility is not the same as market linkage

A stock can be high-risk without being highly correlated with the
market. Boeing is a clear example: its risk is largely **idiosyncratic**
(company-specific events like production issues) rather than driven by
broad market swings. This is why investors need both beta and
correlation when thinking about portfolio exposure.
:::

## Correlation Heatmap

```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-heatmap
#| fig-width: 8.8
#| fig-height: 5.0
par(mar = c(6, 6, 4, 2))

image(
  1:ncol(finance.corr),
  1:nrow(finance.corr),
  t(finance.corr[nrow(finance.corr):1, ]),
  col = colorRampPalette(c("#16384f", "#fbf7ef", "#b84d32"))(100),
  zlim = c(-1, 1),
  axes = FALSE,
  xlab = "",
  ylab = "",
  main = "Return correlation heatmap"
)

axis(1, at = 1:ncol(finance.corr), labels = colnames(finance.corr), las = 2)
axis(2, at = 1:nrow(finance.corr), labels = rev(rownames(finance.corr)), las = 2)

for (i in 1:nrow(finance.corr)) {
  for (j in 1:ncol(finance.corr)) {
    text(j, nrow(finance.corr) - i + 1, labels = sprintf("%.2f", finance.corr[i, j]), cex = 0.8)
  }
}

par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Diversification story

Warmer colors mean stronger positive correlation. JNJ stands out in the
coldest colors â€” it is nearly uncorrelated with every other asset,
including the S&P 500. **Diversification benefit is weakest when all
assets move together**, so a portfolio including JNJ gets more
diversification than one made of only financially- or
industrially-linked names.
:::

## Cumulative Returns

```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-cumret
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4.8
matplot(
  index(finance.cum),
  coredata(finance.cum),
  type = "l",
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  col = 1:7,
  xlab = "Date",
  ylab = "Growth of $1",
  main = "Cumulative Returns"
)

legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = colnames(finance.cum),
  col = 1:7,
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  bty = "n"
)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Path matters, not just average

`r finance.best.cum` finishes with the strongest growth path, while
`r finance.worst.cum` trails the group. Average return alone can hide
very different investment experiences â€” a volatile path to the same
endpoint feels very different from a smooth one.
:::

## Alpha, Beta, and R-Squared

```{r}
#| label: tbl-finance-capm
kable(finance.capm.display, row.names = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Interpreting CAPM parameters

- **Beta** measures sensitivity to the market. $\beta > 1$ means
  amplified market moves; $\beta < 1$ means defensiveness.
- **Alpha** measures excess return beyond what the market predicts
  (Jensen's alpha).
- **R-squared** measures how much of the stock's movement is explained
  by the market factor.

`r finance.high.beta` is the most market-sensitive name here, and
`r finance.high.rsq` has the tightest market linkage.
:::

## CAPM Fitted vs Actual Returns

```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-capm
#| fig-width: 9.5
#| fig-height: 5.0
par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(4, 3.5, 3, 1))

for (ticker in finance.stocks$Ticker) {
  model <- lm(finance.ret.mat[, ticker] ~ finance.ret.mat[, "SP500"])
  plot(
    fitted(model),
    finance.ret.mat[, ticker],
    pch = 16,
    col = adjustcolor("#2a6f97", alpha.f = 0.35),
    cex = 0.6,
    xlab = "CAPM Fitted",
    ylab = "Actual Return",
    main = ticker,
    cex.main = 1
  )
  abline(0, 1, col = "#b83227", lwd = 1.5, lty = 2)
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

## Rolling 60-Day Beta

```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-rollbeta
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4.8
par(mar = c(7, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))

matplot(
  index(finance.roll.beta),
  coredata(finance.roll.beta),
  type = "l",
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  col = 1:6,
  xlab = "Date",
  ylab = "Rolling 60-day beta",
  main = "Rolling beta relative to the S&P 500"
)

legend(
  "bottom",
  inset = c(0, -0.28),
  legend = colnames(finance.roll.beta),
  col = 1:6,
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  horiz = TRUE,
  xpd = NA,
  bty = "n"
)

par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Beta is not constant

A single full-sample beta hides time variation. Rolling beta shows
whether market exposure is stable (like JPM) or highly regime-dependent
(like BA during production crises). Beta above 1 means the stock moved
more aggressively than the market in that 60-day window.
:::

## Rolling 60-Day Correlation

```{r}
#| label: fig-finance-rollcorr
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4.8
par(mar = c(7, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))

matplot(
  index(finance.roll.corr),
  coredata(finance.roll.corr),
  type = "l",
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  col = 1:6,
  xlab = "Date",
  ylab = "Rolling 60-day correlation",
  main = "Rolling correlation with the S&P 500"
)

legend(
  "bottom",
  inset = c(0, -0.28),
  legend = colnames(finance.roll.corr),
  col = 1:6,
  lty = 1,
  lwd = 2,
  horiz = TRUE,
  xpd = NA,
  bty = "n"
)

par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Decomposing beta

Beta combines correlation and relative volatility:
$\beta = \rho \cdot (\sigma_{\text{stock}} / \sigma_{\text{market}})$.
Comparing rolling beta and rolling correlation separates "moves with
the market" from "moves more violently than the market." If beta rises
but correlation stays flat, the stock got more volatile. If both rise,
the stock got more market-linked.
:::

# Final Takeaways

::: {.callout-important}
## Key findings

- **HousePrices:** Centered derived and interaction candidates enter
  before AIC/CV. The final post-Cook model uses
  `r house.clean.ladder$steps$step[house.clean.final.idx]` and keeps
  `r collapse_terms(house.selected.derived)` as derived terms plus
  `r collapse_terms(house.selected.interactions)` as interactions.
  VIF is documented but not used as a pre-filter. Box-Cox is treated as
  `r ifelse(house.boxcox$profile$identity.in.interval, "a check supporting the original price scale", "sensitivity evidence for a transformed response")`.

- **CASchools:** The final post-Cook model uses
  `r schools.clean.ladder$steps$step[schools.clean.final.idx]`, keeps
  `r collapse_terms(schools.selected.derived)` as derived terms and
  `r collapse_terms(schools.selected.interactions)` as interactions.
  Student composition (poverty, income, district type) dominates
  instructional resource variables. Box-Cox is
  `r ifelse(schools.boxcox$profile$identity.in.interval, "not strong enough to replace the original score scale", "useful sensitivity evidence but harder to explain than the original score scale")`.

- **Finance:** The six stocks occupy very different risk-return
  positions, show different market linkage to the S&P 500, and that
  linkage changes over time in the rolling beta and rolling correlation
  plots.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Methodological lessons

Throughout all three analyses, the key methodological lesson is that
proper model selection requires (1) holding out test data until after
all modeling decisions are locked in, (2) checking sensitivity to
influential observations via Cook's distance and verifying the full
curve shifts â€” not just one lucky step, and (3) verifying assumptions
with formal diagnostic tests and bootstrap inference â€” not just
maximizing $R^2$.
:::
